The apostle tells us in Hebrews 7:3 that the Bible doesn’t mention anything about Melchizedek’s family background. This silence points us toward someone greater—Jesus—who has no beginning or end.

Bread and wine were good food for Abram’s tired soldiers. It’s interesting that Jesus used bread and wine too, as symbols of his body and blood, which nourish our souls deeply.

Melchizedek blessed Abram in God’s name. And he praised God using Abram’s name. We should thank God for others’ blessings as if they were our own. Jesus, our great High Priest, helps us pray and praise God. Not only does he offer our prayers to God, but he also prays for us himself.

Abram gave Melchizedek one-tenth of what he captured. When we receive big blessings from God, it’s right to show our gratitude through acts of kindness. Just like Jesus, who is our great Melchizedek, we should honor him by giving him everything we have—not just a small part, but all of it.

14.o: Observe the king of Sodom's grateful offer to Abram, Give me the souls, and take thou the substance. Gratitude teaches us to recompense to the utmost of our power, those that have undergone fatigues, run hazards, and been at expense for our service and benefit. Abram generously refused this offer. He accompanies his refusal with a good reason, Lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich: which would reflect upon the promise promise and covenant of God, as if He would not have enriched Abraham without the spoils of Sodom. The people of God must, for their credit's sake, take heed of doing any thing that looks mean or mercenary, or that savors of covetousness and self-seeking. Abraham can trust the Possessor of Heaven and earth to provide for him.
14.m:
Notice how the king of Sodom offered to give Abram the people while keeping the goods for himself. Being thankful means we should repay others who helped us by taking risks or spending money on our behalf. 

Abram kindly turned down the offer. He explained why he refused, saying, "Lest you say, 'I made Abram rich,' which might suggest that God’s promise to bless him came from the spoils of Sodom." 

God’s followers need to be careful about doing anything that seems selfish or greedy. They shouldn’t do things just to get richer. Abraham trusted that the One who owns heaven and earth would take care of him.

15.o: God assured Abram of safety and happiness; that he should for ever be safe. I am thy shield; or, I am a shield to thee, present with thee, actually caring for thee. The consideration that God himself is, and will be a shield to his people, to secure them from all evils, a shield ready to them, and a shield round about them, should silence all perplexing, tormenting fears.
15.m: God promised Abram safety and happiness. He would always be protected. I am your shield, which means I am here with you, taking care of you. Knowing that God is a shield for his people, protecting them from all harm, should help quiet any worries or fears.

15.o: Though we must never complain of God, yet we have leave to complain to him; and to state all our grievances. It is ease to a burdened spirit, to open its case to a faithful and compassionate friend. Abram's complaint is, that he had no child; that he was never likely to have any; that the want of a son was so great a trouble to him, that it took away all his comfort. If we suppose that Abram looked no further than outward comfort, this complaint was to be blamed. But if we suppose that Abram herein had reference to the promised Seed, his desire was very commendable. Till we have evidence of our interest in Christ, we should not rest satisfied; what will all avail me, if I go Christless? If we continue instant in prayer, yet pray with humble submission to the Divine will, we shall not seek in vain. God gave Abram an express promise of a son. Christians may believe in God with respect to the common concerns of this life; but the faith by which they are justified, always has respect to the person and work of Christ. Abram believed in God as promising Christ; they believe in him as having raised him from the dead, Ro 4:24. Through faith in his blood they obtain forgiveness of sins.
15.m:
We shouldn’t complain about God, but we can tell him our problems. It helps to talk to someone who cares when you’re feeling down. 

Abram told God he had no children and didn’t think he would ever have one. He felt sad because he needed a son. If Abram only cared about having a family here on earth, his worry wasn’t right. But if he wanted God’s special promise about a future descendant, then his concern made sense. Without Jesus, nothing else really matters. 

If we keep praying humbly and asking for what we need, God won’t let us search in vain. 

God promised Abram he would have a son. We can trust God for things in our lives, but our main hope should always be in Jesus. Abram trusted God’s promise about Jesus. Today, we believe God raised Jesus from the dead and through him get forgiven sins.

15.o: Assurance was given to Abram of the land of Canaan for an inheritance. God never promises more than he is able to perform, as men often do. Abram did as God commanded him. He divided the beasts in the midst, according to the ceremony used in confirming covenants, Jer 34:18, 19. Having prepared according to God's appointment, he set himself to wait for the sign God might give him. A watch must be kept upon our spiritual sacrifices. When vain thoughts, like these fowls, come down upon our sacrifices, we must drive them away, and seek to attend on God without distraction.
15.m:
God promised Abraham that Canaan would belong to him. God always keeps his promises, unlike people who sometimes promise things they can't deliver. 

Abraham followed God’s instructions. He split the animals in half, just like the ceremony for making agreements, as seen in Jeremiah 34:18, 19. After preparing everything as God told him, he waited for God to show him a sign.

We need to stay alert while offering our prayers and good deeds to God. When distracting thoughts, like those birds, try to interfere with our worship, we should push them away so we can focus on praying without interruptions.

15.o: A deep sleep fell upon Abram; with this sleep a horror of great darkness fell upon him: a sudden change. The children of light do not always walk in the light. Several things were then foretold. 1. The suffering state of Abram's seed for a long time. They shall be strangers. The heirs of heaven are strangers on earth. They shall be servants; but Canaanites serve under a curse, the Hebrews under a blessing. They shall be suffers. Those that are blessed and beloved of God, are often sorely afflicted by wicked men. 2. The judgment of the enemies of Abram's seed. Though God may allow persecutors and oppressors to trample upon his people a great while, he will certainly reckon with them at last. 3. That great event, the deliverance of Abram's seed out of Egypt, is here foretold. 4. Their happy settlement in Canaan. They shall come hither again. The measure of sin fills gradually. Some people's measure of sin fills slowly. The knowledge of future events would seldom add to our comfort. In the most favoured families, and most happy lives, there are so many afflictions, that it is merciful in God to conceal what will befall us and ours.
15.m:
A deep sleep came over Abraham. With this sleep, a feeling of great fear and darkness came upon him. A sudden change happened. People who love God don’t always live in joy all the time.

Several things were told to Abraham. 

First, about the hard times his family would face for a long time. His descendants would be strangers in other lands. People who belong in heaven feel like strangers on Earth. They would also be slaves, but the Canaanites were cursed, while the Hebrews had a blessing. They would suffer much. Even though God’s loved ones might face terrible treatment from evil people, God still cares for them.

Second, about the punishment for those who hurt Abraham’s family. Although God lets bad people harm his followers for a long time, he will punish them eventually.

Third, about freeing Abraham’s family from slavery in Egypt. This was predicted too.

Fourth, about their return to Canaan. They would come back home. Sin builds up little by little. For some, it happens quickly, while for others, it takes longer. Knowing future troubles doesn’t usually help us feel better. Even in the best families and happiest lives, there are many problems. It’s kind of God to keep these future troubles hidden from us.

15.o: The smoking furnace and the burning lamp, probably represented the Israelites' severe trials and joyful deliverance, with their gracious supports in the mean time. It is probable that this furnace and lamp, which passed between the pieces, burned and consumed them, and so completed the sacrifice, and testified God's acceptance of it. So it intimates that God's covenants with man are made by sacrifice, Ps 50:5. And we may know that he accepts our sacrifices, if he kindles in our souls pious and devout affections. The bounds of the land granted are stated. Several nations, or tribes, are spoken of, that must be cast out to make room for the seed of Abram. In this chapter we perceive in Abram faith struggling against, and triumphing over, unbelief. Wonder not, believers, if you meet with seasons of darkness and distress. But it is not the will of God that you should be cast down: fear not; for all that he was to Abram he will be to you.
15.m:
The smoking furnace and the burning lamp likely showed the hard times and happy endings the Israelites would have, along with God’s help during those times. 

It seems the furnace and lamp that went between the pieces burned them up, finishing the sacrifice and showing God accepted it. This shows that God makes his promises with people through sacrifices, as in Psalm 50:5. We know God accepts our offerings if he lights a fire of devotion in us.

God tells the boundaries of the land given to Abram. He mentions several other groups who need to leave to make space for Abram’s family.

In this part, we see Abram’s faith growing stronger even when doubts come. Don’t be surprised, believers, if you face dark and tough times. But don’t worry—God doesn’t want you to feel down. Trust him because whatever he did for Abram, he’ll do for you too.

16.o: Sarai, no longer expecting to have children herself, proposed to Abram to take another wife, whose children she might; her slave, whose children would be her property. This was done without asking counsel of the Lord. Unbelief worked, God's almighty power was forgotten. It was a bad example, and a source of manifold uneasiness. In every relation and situation in life there is some cross for us to bear: much of the exercise of faith consists in patiently submitting, in waiting the Lord's time, and using only those means which he appoints for the removal of the cross. Foul temptations may have very fair pretences, and be coloured with that which is very plausible. Fleshly wisdom puts us out of God's way. This would not be the case, if we would ask counsel of God by his word and by prayer, before we attempt that which is doubtful.
16.m:
Sarai, thinking she could no longer have children on her own, suggested to Abram that he marry Hagar, her servant. Sarai thought Hagar’s children would belong to her. They did this without seeking advice from God. Their lack of faith made them forget about God’s great power. 

This was a bad choice that caused many problems. In all parts of our lives, we face challenges. Faith often means being patient and trusting God’s timing. We should use only the methods God gives us to solve our problems.

Bad ideas can seem good at first. Human wisdom can lead us away from God. If we talk to God through His Word and prayers before making decisions, we won’t go wrong.

16.o: Abram's unhappy marriage to Hagar very soon made a great deal of mischief. We may thank ourselves for the guilt and grief that follow us, when we go out of the way of our duty. See it in this case, Passionate people often quarrel with others, for things of which they themselves must bear the blame. Sarai had given her maid to Abram, yet she cries out, My wrong be upon thee. That is never said wisely, which pride and anger put into our mouths. Those are not always in the right, who are most loud and forward in appealing to God: such rash and bold imprecations commonly speak guilt and a bad cause. Hagar forgot that she herself had first given the provocation, by despising her mistress. Those that suffer for their faults, ought to bear it patiently, 1Pe 2:20.
16.m:
Abram’s unhappy marriage to Hagar caused a lot of trouble. We bring on ourselves the guilt and sadness that come from going against what we should do. In this case, Sarai gave her servant Hagar to Abram, but then complained, “My wrong is on you.” Saying angry things isn’t wise. People who yell about being treated unfairly might actually be at fault. 

Sarai accused Abram loudly, but her quick words showed she was likely guilty too. Hagar forgot that she had been disrespectful to Sarai first. When we get in trouble because of our mistakes, we should accept it calmly, as Peter wrote in 1 Peter 2:20.

16.o: Hagar was out of her place, and out of the way of her duty, and going further astray, when the Angel found her. It is a great mercy to be stopped in a sinful way, either by conscience or by providence. Whence comest thou? Consider that thou art running from duty, and the privileges thou wast blest with in Abram's tent. It is good to live in a religious family, which those ought to consider who have this advantage. Whither wilt thou go? Thou art running into sin; if Hagar return to Egypt, she will return to idol gods, and into danger in the wilderness through which she must travel. Recollecting who we are, would often teach us our duty. Inquiring whence we came, would show us our sin and folly. Considering whither we shall go, discovers our danger and misery. And those who leave their space and duty, must hasten their return, how mortifying soever it be. The declaration of the Angel, “I will,” shows this Angel was the eternal Word and Son of God. Hagar could not but admire the Lord's mercy, and feel, Have I, who am so unworthy, been favoured with a gracious visit from the Lord? She was brought to a better temper, returned, and by her behaviour softened Sarai, and received more gentle treatment. Would that we were always suitably impressed with this thought, Thou God seest me!
16.m:
Hagar was far from where she should be, neglecting her duties and wandering farther away when the angel found her. It’s a blessing to be stopped on a wrong path, whether by your own conscience or by events beyond your control.

“Where are you coming from?” Think about it—by leaving Abraham’s home, you’re abandoning your duties and the blessings you had there. Living in a godly household is a privilege, one that people should value.

“Where do you plan to go?” You’re heading toward trouble. If Hagar goes back to Egypt, she’ll end up worshiping false gods and face dangers along the way. Remembering who we are often reminds us what we should do. Looking at where we’ve come from helps us see our mistakes. Thinking about where we’re headed makes us realize our risks and troubles.

Those who abandon their responsibilities need to quickly turn around, even if it’s hard for them. When the angel said, “I will,” it showed he was the eternal Word and Son of God. Hagar marveled at God’s kindness, wondering, “How can someone like me receive such grace?”

She calmed down, went back, and acted kindly toward Sarah, earning gentler treatment herself. May we always remember, “God sees me.”

17.o: The covenant was to be accomplished in due time. The promised Seed was Christ, and Christians in him. And all who are of faith are blessed with faithful Abram, being partakers of the same covenant blessings. In token of this covenant his name was changed from Abram, “a high father,” to Abraham, “the father of a multitude.” All that the Christian world enjoys, it is indebted for to Abraham and his Seed.
17.m: The promise would come true at the right time. The promised descendant was Jesus, and those who believe in him are part of that promise too. People of faith share the same blessings as faithful Abraham. As a sign of this promise, his name was changed from Abram, which means "high father," to Abraham, which means "father of many." Everything good that Christians have today comes from Abraham and his descendants.

17.o: The covenant of grace is from everlasting in the counsels of it, and to everlasting in the consequences of it. The token of the covenant was circumcision. It is here said to be the covenant which Abraham and his seed must keep. Those who will have the Lord to be to them a God, must resolve to be to him a people. Not only Abraham and Isaac, and his posterity by Isaac, were to be circumcised, but also Ishmael and the bond-servants. It sealed not only the covenant of the land of Canaan to Isaac's posterity, but of heaven, through Christ, to the whole church of God. The outward sign is for the visible church; the inward seal of the Spirit is peculiar to those whom God knows to be believers, and he alone can know them. The religious observance of this institution was required, under a very severe penalty. It is dangerous to make light of Divine institutions, and to live in the neglect of them. The covenant in question was one that involved great blessings for the world in all future ages. Even the blessedness of Abraham himself, and all the rewards conferred upon him, were for Christ's sake. Abraham was justified, as we have seen, not by his own righteousness, but by faith in the promised Messiah.
17.m:
The covenant of grace has always been part of God’s plans and will last forever. Circumcision was the sign of this covenant. Here, it is called the covenant that Abraham and his descendants must follow. To have God as their God, people must agree to be His people. 

Not just Abraham, Isaac, and Isaac’s children had to be circumcised, but also Ishmael and the servants. This sign wasn’t just about giving the land of Canaan to Isaac’s family. It was also about promising heaven through Jesus to everyone in God’s church. 

Circumcision showed who belonged to the visible church. But only God knew who truly believed, and He gave them the special seal of the Holy Spirit. People were told to follow this rule carefully, or there would be serious consequences. We should not ignore God’s commands.

This covenant brought many good things for people in all generations. Abraham received blessings because of what Jesus would do. Abraham was made right with God through faith in the coming Messiah, not by doing good works on his own.

17.o: Here is the promise made to Abraham of a son by Sarai, in whom the promise made to him should be fulfilled. The assurance of this promise was the change of Sarai's name into Sarah. Sarai signifies my princess, as if her honour were confined to one family only; Sarah signifies a princess. The more favours God confers upon us, the more low we should be in our own eyes. Abraham showed great joy; he laughed, it was a laughter of delight, not of distrust. Now it was that Abraham rejoiced to see Christ's day; now he saw it and was glad, Joh 8:56. Abraham, dreading lest Ishmael should be abandoned and forsaken of God, put up a petition on his behalf. God gives us leave in prayer to be particular in making known our requests. Whatever is our care and fear, should be spread before God in prayer. It is the duty of parents to pray for their children, and the great thing we should desire is, that they may be kept in covenant with Him, and may have grace to walk before him in uprightness. Common blessings are secured to Ishmael. Outward good things are often given to those children of godly parents who are born after the flesh, for their parents' sake. Covenant blessings are reserved for Isaac, and appropriated to him.
17.m:
Here is the promise made to Abraham about having a son through Sarah, which would fulfill God’s promises to him. To show that this promise was real, God changed Sarah’s name from Sarai. Sarai means “my princess,” suggesting she was important within just one family. But Sarah means “princess” without limits, showing greater honor. 

Abraham felt very happy about this news. He laughed out loud because he was joyful, not doubtful. At this time, Abraham could see Jesus’ future and was glad about it, as John 8:56 tells us.

Abraham worried that Ishmael might be left alone by God. So, he prayed asking God to take care of Ishmael. We can ask God specifically about what we’re concerned about in our prayers. When we worry about our children, we should tell God about it. The most important thing to pray for them is that they stay close to God and live right.

God promised common blessings to Ishmael too. Sometimes, God blesses the children of believers even though they aren’t part of God’s special covenant. But God reserved the covenant blessings for Isaac, giving them only to him.

17.o: Abraham and all his family were circumcised; so receiving the token of the covenant, and distinguishing themselves from other families that had no part nor lot in the matter. It was an implicit obedience; he did as God said unto him, and did not ask why or wherefore. He did it because God bade him. It was a speedy obedience; in the self-same day. Sincere obedience makes no delay. Not only the doctrines of revelation, but the seals of God's covenant, remind us that we are guilty, polluted sinners. They show us our need of the blood of atonement; they point to the promised Saviour, and teach us to exercise faith in him. They show us that without regeneration, and sanctification by his Spirit, and the mortification of our corrupt and carnal inclinations, we cannot be in covenant with God. But let us remember that the true circumcision is that of the heart, by the Spirit, Ro 2:28, 29. Both under the old and new dispensation, many have had the outward profession, and the outward seal, who were never sealed by the Holy Spirit of promise.
17.m:
Abraham and everyone in his household were circumcised. By doing this, they received the sign of the covenant and set themselves apart from other families who were not included. Abraham followed God’s instructions without questioning them. He obeyed because God told him to do it. 

He acted quickly too. He carried out the command on the very same day. When someone obeys sincerely, there is no hesitation. 

Both the teachings revealed through God and the signs of His covenant remind us that we are sinful people in need of forgiveness. These things show us that we require Jesus’ sacrifice for our sins. They help us believe in Him. 

They also tell us that unless we are changed by God’s Spirit and stop living according to our selfish desires, we cannot be in a relationship with God. However, we should remember that real circumcision comes from being transformed inside by the Holy Spirit, as Romans 2:28, 29 explains. 

Under both the old and new ways of worship, some people made public claims about their faith and received physical marks of the covenant, yet they were not truly touched by the Holy Spirit within.

18.o: Abraham was waiting to entertain any weary traveller, for inns were not to be met with as among us. While Abraham was thus sitting, he saw three men coming. These were three heavenly beings in human bodies. Some think they were all created angels; others, that one of them was the Son of God, the Angel of the covenant. Washing the feet is customary in those hot climates, where only sandals are worn. We should not be forgetful to entertain strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares, Heb 13:2; nay, the Lord of angels himself; as we always do, when for his sake we entertain the least of his brethren. Cheerful and obliging manners in showing kindness, are great ornaments to piety. Though our condescending Lord vouchsafes not personal visits to us, yet still by his Spirit he stands at the door and knocks; when we are inclined to open, he deigns to enter; and by his gracious consolations he provides a rich feast, of which we partake with him, Re 3:20.
18.m: Abraham was ready to help anyone who was tired from traveling because there weren’t many places to stay back then. One day while he was sitting outside, he saw three people coming toward him. Some believe these were angels, while others think one of them was actually Jesus in disguise. In hot areas, people often wash their feet since they wear just sandals. We shouldn't ignore strangers because sometimes we might unknowingly welcome an angel. The Bible tells us that by welcoming strangers, we could even be hosting Jesus himself (Hebrews 13:2). Being kind and cheerful when helping others shows true faith. Even though Jesus doesn’t visit us personally anymore, he’s still close to us through his Holy Spirit. He waits for us to let him in, offering comfort and blessings whenever we invite him in (Revelation 3:20).

18.o: Where is Sarah thy wife? was asked. Note the answer, In the tent. Just at hand, in her proper place, occupied in her household concerns. There is nothing got by gadding. Those are most likely to receive comfort from God and his promises, who are in their proper place, and in the way of their duty, Lu 2:8. We are slow of heart to believe, and need line upon line to the same purport. The blessings others have from common providence, believers have from the Divine promise, which makes them very sweet, and very sure. The spiritual seed of Abraham owe their life, and joy, and hope, and all, to the promise. Sarah thinks this too good news to be true; she laughed, and therefore cannot as yet find in her heart to believe it. Sarah laughed. We might not have thought there was a difference between Sarah's laughter and Abraham's, ch. 17:17; but He who searches the heart, saw that the one sprung from unbelief, and the other from faith. She denied that she had laughed. One sin commonly brings in another, and it is not likely we shall strictly keep to truth, when we question the Divine truth. But whom the Lord loves he will rebuke, convict, silence, and bring to repentance, and if they sin before him.
18.m:
Someone asked, "Where is Sarah your wife?" Notice the answer: "In the tent." She’s right nearby, doing what she should be doing around the house. It doesn’t help to wander around aimlessly. People are most likely to get comfort from God’s promises when they’re where they should be and doing what they’re supposed to do, Luke 2:8. 

We often don't believe quickly enough and need to hear things over and over again. Other people may enjoy good things that come naturally, but believers see those same good things as gifts from God’s promises, making them even sweeter and more certain.

The spiritual children of Abraham depend on God’s promises for everything—life, joy, hope, and more. When told such good news, Sarah couldn’t help but laugh because she didn’t think it could be true. So she laughed. 

At first glance, you might not notice a difference between Sarah’s laugh and Abraham’s, chapter 17:17, but God knows our hearts. His sees that one laugh came from doubt while the other came from faith. 

Sarah tried to deny laughing. But when someone sins against God, they usually end up sinning more. And it’s hard to tell the truth when you’ve already doubted God’s word. Yet, God corrects those he loves, bringing them back to repentance.

18.o: The two who are supposed to have been created angels went toward Sodom. The one who is called Jehovah throughout the chapter, continued with Abraham, and would not hide from him the thing he intended to do. Though God long forbears with sinners, from which they fancy that the Lord does not see, and does not regard; yet when the day of his wrath comes, he will look toward them. The Lord will give Abraham an opportunity to intercede with him, and shows him the reason of his conduct. Consider, as a very bright part of Abraham's character and example, that he not only prayed with his family, but he was very careful to teach and rule them well. Those who expect family blessings must make conscience of family duty. Abraham did not fill their heads with matters of doubtful dispute; but he taught them to be serious and devout in the worship of God, and to be honest in their dealings with all men. Of how few may such a character be given in our days! How little care is taken by masters of families to ground those under them in the principles of religion! Do we watch from sabbath to sabbath whether they go forward or backward?
18.m:
Two beings who were originally created as angels headed towards Sodom. One of them, referred to as Jehovah throughout the chapter, stayed with Abraham and didn't hide what he planned to do from him. Even though God waits a long time before punishing sinners, people think he doesn’t notice their actions. But when God’s anger finally comes, he won’t ignore them anymore. 

God gives Abraham a chance to pray for them and explains why he needs to punish Sodom. This shows us a key part of Abraham’s good character—he not only prayed with his family but also made sure to teach them and guide them properly. People who want blessings for their family need to take their duties seriously. 

Abraham didn’t confuse his family with complicated questions. Instead, he taught them to be serious about worshipping God and to be honest with everyone. Not many people today can say the same about themselves. Sadly, most parents don’t spend enough time making sure their children learn about God. We should pay attention each week to see if our family members are growing closer to God or moving away.

18.o: Here is the first solemn prayer upon record in the Bible; and it is a prayer for the sparing of Sodom. Abraham prayed earnestly that Sodom might be spared, if but a few righteous persons should be found in it. Come and learn from Abraham what compassion we should feel for sinners, and how earnestly we should pray for them. We see here that the effectual, fervent prayer of a righteous man avails much. Abraham, indeed, failed in his request for the whole place, but Lot was miraculously delivered. Be encouraged then to expect, by earnest prayer, the blessing of God upon your families, your friends, your neighbourhood. To this end you must not only pray, but you must live like Abraham. He knew the Judge of all the earth would do right. He does not plead that the wicked may be spared for their own sake, or because it would be severe to destroy them, but for the sake of the righteous who might be found among them. And righteousness only can be made a plea before God. How then did Christ make intercession for transgressors? Not by blaming the Divine law, nor by alleging aught in extenuation or excuse of human guilt; but by pleading HIS OWN obedience unto death.
18.m:
Here is the first recorded prayer in the Bible. It’s a prayer asking God to spare Sodom. Abraham prayed very hard that Sodom could be saved if just a few good people were there. Learn from Abraham about feeling sorry for sinners and praying strongly for them. The powerful prayers of good people really work. Even though Abraham didn’t get everything he asked for, Lot was still saved through a miracle. So keep praying for blessings on your family, friends, and community. But remember, you need to live like Abraham too. 

Abraham trusted that God always does what is fair. He didn't ask God to save the bad people, but rather to protect the good ones who might be there. Only goodness can be used to talk to God. Think about how Jesus prayed for those who hurt him. He didn’t blame God’s rules or try to excuse what people did wrong. Instead, he talked about his own obedience to God.

19.o: Lot was good, but there was not one more of the same character in the city. All the people of Sodom were very wicked and vile. Care was therefore taken for saving Lot and his family. Lot lingered; he trifled. Thus many who are under convictions about their spiritual state, and the necessity of a change, defer that needful work. The salvation of the most righteous men is of God's mercy, not by their own merit. We are saved by grace. God's power also must be acknowledged in bringing souls out of a sinful state If God had not been merciful to us, our lingering had been our ruin. Lot must flee for his life. He must not hanker after Sodom. Such commands as these are given to those who, through grace, are delivered out of a sinful state and condition. Return not to sin and Satan. Rest not in self and the world. Reach toward Christ and heaven, for that is escaping to the mountain, short of which we must not stop. Concerning this destruction, observe that it is a revelation of the wrath of God against sin and sinners of all ages. Let us learn from hence the evil of sin, and its hurtful nature; it leads to ruin.
19.m:
Lot was good, but there wasn’t anyone else like him in the city. Everyone in Sodom was very wicked. So care was taken to save Lot and his family. 

Lot hesitated. He delayed. Many people who know they need to change their ways often put off doing so. Saving even the best people depends on God’s mercy, not their worthiness. We are saved by God’s grace. 

God’s power must also be recognized in rescuing people from sin. Without God’s mercy, our delays would have ruined us. 

Lot needed to leave quickly for safety. He shouldn’t look back at Sodom. This command applies to people who, through God’s grace, escape from a sinful way of life. Don’t return to sin and Satan. Don’t rest in yourself or the world. Aim for Christ and heaven, because that means fleeing to safety. 

This destruction shows the anger of God against sin and sinners throughout history. It teaches us how harmful sin is and how it leads to ruin.

19.o: See the peril of security. Lot, who kept chaste in Sodom, and was a mourner for the wickedness of the place, and a witness against it, when in the mountain, alone, and, as he thought, out of the way of temptation, is shamefully overtaken. Let him that thinks he stands high, and stands firm, take heed lest he fall. See the peril of drunkenness; it is not only a great sin itself, but lets in many sins, which bring a lasting wound and dishonour. Many a man does that, when he is drunk, which, when he is sober, he could not think of without horror. See also the peril of temptation, even from relations and friends, whom we love and esteem, and expect kindness from. We must dread a snare, wherever we are, and be always upon our guard. No excuse can be made for the daughters, nor for Lot. Scarcely any account can be given of the affair but this, The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked: who can know it? From the silence of the Scripture concerning Lot henceforward, learn that drunkenness, as it makes men forgetful, so it makes them to be forgotten.
19.m:
See the danger of feeling too safe. Lot stayed pure in Sodom, even though he lived among bad people. He tried to stay away from trouble by going up on a mountain. But there, alone and thinking himself safe, he got caught doing shameful things. People who think they’re strong should watch out because they might fall.

Drinking too much alcohol is very dangerous. It’s not just wrong in itself, but it leads to other sins that cause long-lasting harm and shame. When someone drinks too much, they do things while drunk that would horrify them if they were sober.

Also, beware of being tempted by those you trust, such as family members and friends. They may try to lead you astray. Always be cautious and ready to defend yourself.

Neither Lot’s daughters nor he had an excuse for their actions. Their hearts led them to do evil things. Only God knows what goes on inside us.

The Bible doesn’t mention Lot again after this event. This shows that drinking makes people forgetful and causes others to forget about them too.

20.o: Crooked policy will not prosper: it brings ourselves and others into danger. God gives Abimelech notice of his danger of sin, and his danger of death for his sin. Every wilful sinner is a dead man, but Abimelech pleads ignorance. If our consciences witness, that, however we may have been cheated into a snare, we have not knowingly sinned against God, it will be our rejoicing in the day of evil. It is matter of comfort to those who are honest, that God knows their honesty, and will acknowledge it. It is a great mercy to be hindered from committing sin; of this God must have the glory. But if we have ignorantly done wrong, that will not excuse us, if we knowingly persist in it. He that does wrong, whoever he is, prince or peasant, shall certainly receive for the wrong which he has done, unless he repent, and, if possible, make restitution.
20.m:
Crooked plans won't work well. They put us and others in danger. God warns Abimelech about the trouble his sins will cause him, and the punishment he'll face because of them. Anyone who keeps on doing bad things is heading towards trouble, but Abimelech claims he didn’t know what he was doing was wrong. 

If our conscience tells us we were tricked into doing something sinful without knowing it was wrong, we can feel better on the day of judgment. People who are honest can take comfort knowing God sees their honesty and will recognize it. Being stopped from doing wrong is a big blessing, and we should thank God for it. However, if we did something wrong out of ignorance, we still need to fix it if we learn it was wrong. Ignorance doesn’t excuse us if we keep doing it after we find out it’s wrong.

No matter who you are—a king or just an ordinary person—you’ll get punished for the wrongs you do, unless you change your ways and try to make up for them.

20.o: See here much to blame, even in the father of the faithful. Mark his distrust of God, his undue care about life, his intent to deceive. He also threw temptation in the way of others, caused affliction to them, exposed himself and Sarah to just rebukes, and yet attempted an excuse. These things are written for our warning, not for us to imitate. Even Abraham hath not whereof to glory. He cannot be justified by his works, but must be indebted for justification, to that righteousness which is upon all and unto all them that believe. We must not condemn all as hypocrites who fall into sin, if they do not continue in it. But let the unhumbled and impenitent take heed that they do not sin on, thinking that grace may abound. Abimelech, being warned of God, takes the warning; and being truly afraid of sin and its consequences, he rose early to pursue the directions given him.
20.m:
Here we see some mistakes even from the father of the faithful. Notice his lack of trust in God, his worry about his safety, and his plan to trick others. He put people in danger, caused trouble for them, brought shame on himself and Sarah, and tried to cover up his mistake. 

These stories teach us what not to do. Even great men like Abraham aren’t perfect. They can't earn their salvation through good deeds. Instead, they need to rely on God’s grace, which is available to everyone who believes.

We shouldn’t judge everyone who makes a mistake as a hypocrite, especially if they don’t keep doing wrong things. But those who refuse to admit fault should beware of continuing to sin, thinking they’ll get away with it.

When Abimelech was told by God, he listened right away. Because he was truly scared of sinning, he got up early to follow God’s instructions.

20.o: We often trouble ourselves, and even are led into temptation and sin, by groundless suspicions; and find the fear of God where we expected it not. Agreements to deceive generally end in shame and sorrow; and restraints from sin, though by suffering, should be thankfully acknowledged. Though the Lord rebuke, yet he will pardon and deliver his people, and he will give them favour in the sight of those with whom they sojourn; and overrule their infirmities, when they are humbled for them, so that they shall prove useful to themselves and others.
20.m: We often worry needlessly and get tempted to do wrong because of unfounded doubts. We sometimes discover God’s protection where we least expect it. Plans to trick others usually lead to embarrassment and regret. When we avoid doing evil, even if it causes us pain, we should be grateful. Even when God corrects us, he still forgives and rescues his people. He helps them earn respect from those around them. And when we admit our mistakes, God uses them to help us and others grow.

21.o: Few under the Old Testament were brought into the world with such expectations as Isaac. He was in this a type of Christ, that Seed which the holy God so long promised, and holy men so long expected. He was born according to the promise, at the set time of which God had spoken. God's promised mercies will certainly come at the time which He sets, and that is the best time. Isaac means “laughter,” and there was good reason for the name, ch. 17:17; 18:13. When the Sun of comfort is risen upon the soul, it is good to remember how welcome the dawning of the day was. When Sarah received the promise, she laughed with distrust and doubt. When God gives us the mercies we began to despair of, we ought to remember with sorrow and shame our sinful distrust of his power and promise, when we were in pursuit of them. This mercy filled Sarah with joy and wonder. God's favours to his covenant people are such as surpass their own and others' thoughts and expectations: who could imagine that he should do so much for those that deserve so little, nay, for those that deserve so ill? Who would have said that God should send his Son to die for us, his Spirit to make us holy, his angels to attend us? Who would have said that such great sins should be pardoned, such mean services accepted, and such worthless worms taken into covenant? A short account of Isaac's infancy is given. God's blessing upon the nursing of children, and the preservation of them through the perils of the infant age, are to be acknowledged as signal instances of the care and tenderness of the Divine providence. See Ps 22:9, 10; Ho 11:1, 2.
21.m:
Few people under the Old Testament were born with more hope than Isaac. He was like Jesus, the promised seed that everyone waited for. 

Isaac was born just as God had promised, at the exact time God said he would be. God’s promises always come true on schedule. The best time is whatever time God chooses.

His name meant “laughter.” There was a good reason for the name. When the light of happiness comes into someone’s life, it’s nice to think about how happy you were when things started getting better.

When Sarah first heard the promise, she laughed because she didn’t believe it. But now that God has kept His word, we should feel sorry for doubting Him before.

Sarah was very happy and amazed by what God did. God’s gifts to His chosen people often surprise us. How could anyone guess that God would do so much for people who don’t deserve it? People who even sin against Him?

God sent His Son to die for us, His Spirit to make us holy, and His angels to protect us. Who would have thought that such big sins would be forgiven, small acts of service would be accepted, and lowly people would be part of God’s family?

The story tells us a bit about Isaac’s early years. We should thank God for taking care of children and keeping them safe from danger. Psalm 22 talks about this. So does Hosea 11.

God watches over babies and keeps them safe. That shows how kind and caring God is.

21.o: Let us not overlook the manner in which this family matter instructs us not to rest in outward privileges, or in our own doings. And let us seek the blessings of the new covenant by faith in its Divine Surety. Ishmael's conduct was persecution, being done in profane contempt of the covenant and promise, and with malice against Isaac. God takes notice of what children say and do in their play; and will reckon with them, if they say or do amiss, though their parents do not. Mocking is a great sin, and very provoking to God. And the children of promise must expect to be mocked. Abraham was grieved that Ishmael should misbehave, and Sarah demand so severe a punishment. But God showed him that Isaac must be the father of the promised Seed; therefore, send Ishmael away, lest he corrupt the manners, or try to take the rights of Isaac. The covenant seed of Abraham must be a people by themselves, not mingled with those who were out of covenant: Sarah little thought of this; but God turned aright what she said.
21.m:
Don't miss how this family story teaches us not to rely on just having special privileges or doing good things ourselves. Instead, we should look for the blessings of the new covenant through faith in Jesus Christ.

Ishmael’s actions were mean-spirited, showing disrespect toward the covenant and mocking his brother Isaac. God sees what kids say and do, even during playtime. He holds them accountable if they act wrongly, whether their parents notice it or not. Mockery is a serious sin and makes God angry.

The children chosen by God can expect to face ridicule too. Abraham felt sad about Ishmael’s behavior and Sarah’s harsh request. But God told him that Isaac had to be the one carrying on the promise. So, Abraham needed to send Ishmael away to prevent him from influencing Isaac badly or trying to take his place. 

God wanted the descendants of Abraham to form a separate group, distinct from others outside the covenant. Sarah didn’t realize this at first, but God made sure her words led to the right outcome.

21.o: If Hagar and Ishmael had behaved well in Abraham's family, they might have continued there; but they were justly punished. By abusing privileges, we forfeit them. Those who know not when they are well off, will be made to know the worth of mercies by the want of them. They were brought to distress in the wilderness. It is not said that the provisions were spent, or that Abraham sent them away without money. But the water was spent; and having lost their way, in that hot climate Ishmael was soon overcome with fatigue and thirst. God's readiness to help us when we are in trouble, must not slacken, but quicken our endeavours to help ourselves. The promise concerning her son is repeated, as a reason why Hagar should bestir herself to help him. It should engage our care and pains about children and young people, to consider that we know not what great use God has designed them for, and may make of them. The angel directs her to a present supply. Many who have reason to be comforted, go mourning from day to day, because they do not see the reason they have for comfort. There is a well of water near them in the covenant of grace, but they are not aware of it, till the same God that opened their eyes to see their wound, opens them to see their remedy. Paran was a wild place, fit for a wild man; such as Ishmael. Those who are born after the flesh, take up with the wilderness of this world, while the children of the promise aim at the heavenly Canaan, and cannot be at rest till they are there. Yet God was with the lad; his outward welfare was owing to this.
21.m:
If Hagar and Ishmael had been good in Abraham’s family, they could have stayed. But they were punished fairly. When we misuse our privileges, we lose them. People who don’t know when they’re lucky often learn the value of blessings only when those blessings are gone.

They faced hardship in the desert. The story doesn’t say their food ran out or that Abraham sent them away without money. But their water was used up, and in the scorching heat, Ishmael became very tired and thirsty.

God helps us when we need it, which should encourage us to help ourselves too. Thinking about how important God plans to make our children should motivate us to take care of them. 

The angel told Hagar where to find water nearby. Some people feel sad even though they have reasons to be happy, simply because they don’t recognize those reasons. Just like how God showed Hagar the well, he shows us solutions to our problems.

Paran was a wild place, fitting for someone like Ishmael. Physical-born people settle for the world’s wilderness, while promised children look forward to heaven. Still, God watched over the boy, taking care of his needs.

21.o: Abimelech felt sure that the promises of God would be fulfilled to Abraham. It is wise to connect ourselves with those who are blessed of God; and we ought to requite kindness to those who have been kind to us. Wells of water are scarce and valuable in eastern countries. Abraham took care to have his title to the well allowed, to prevent disputes in future. No more can be expected from an honest man than that he be ready to do right, as soon as he knows he has done wrong. Abraham, being now in a good neighbourhood, stayed a great while there. There he made, not only a constant practice, but an open profession of his religion. There he called on the name of the Lord, as the everlasting God; probably in the grove he planted, which was his place of prayer. Abraham kept up public worship, in which his neighbours might join. Good men should do all they can to make others so. Wherever we sojourn, we must neither neglect nor be ashamed of the worship of Jehovah.
21.m:
Abimelech believed that God’s promises to Abraham would come true. It’s smart to connect with people whom God blesses. We should repay those who have been kind to us. 

Wells of water are rare and important in places like the East. Abraham wanted to make sure everyone knew he owned the well to avoid problems later. An honest person will try to fix things once they know they’ve done something wrong.

Since Abraham was now in a friendly area, he stayed for a long time. He openly practiced and talked about his faith. He likely prayed at the grove he planted, calling out to the eternal God. 

Abraham held regular worship services where neighbors could join. Good people should help others become good too. Whenever we stay somewhere new, we shouldn’t ignore or hide our worship of Jehovah.

22.o: We never are secure from trials In Hebrew, to tempt, and to try, or to prove, are expressed by the same word. Every trial is indeed a temptation, and tends to show the dispositions of the heart, whether holy or unholy. But God proved Abraham, not to draw him to sin, as Satan tempts. Strong faith is often exercised with strong trials, and put upon hard services. The command to offer up his son, is given in such language as makes the trial more grievous; every word here is a sword. Observe, 1. The person to be offered: Take thy son; not thy bullocks and thy lambs. How willingly would Abraham have parted with them all to redeem Isaac! Thy son; not thy servant. Thine only son; thine only son by Sarah. Take Isaac, that son whom thou lovest. 2. The place: three days' journey off; so that Abraham might have time to consider, and might deliberately obey. 3. The manner: Offer him for a burnt-offering; not only kill his son, his Isaac, but kill him as a sacrifice; kill him with all that solemn pomp and ceremony, with which he used to offer his burnt-offerings.
22.m:
We are never safe from tests. In Hebrew, the words for "to test" and "to tempt" are the same. Every test is a kind of temptation, showing what’s really in our hearts—good or bad. But God tested Abraham without trying to lead him to do wrong, unlike Satan who tries to get us to sin.

Strong faith is often tested with big challenges. When God told Abraham to offer his son, He made the task very difficult. Each word was like a sharp blade.

Notice these points:
1. The person to offer: “Take your son,” not just animals you could give away easily. Abraham would gladly have given everything else to save Isaac. “Your son,” not some slave. “Your only son,” the one born to Sarah. “Isaac, the son you love.”
2. The location: Three days’ travel away. This gave Abraham time to think about it carefully before following through.
3. The way to do it: Offer him as a burnt offering. Not just kill Isaac, but do it as if he were a sacrifice. Kill him with all the usual ceremonies used for sacrifices.

22.o: Never was any gold tried in so hot a fire. Who but Abraham would not have argued with God? Such would have been the thought of a weak heart; but Abraham knew that he had to do with a God, even Jehovah. Faith had taught him not to argue, but to obey. He is sure that what God commands is good; that what he promises cannot be broken. In matters of God, whoever consults with flesh and blood, will never offer up his Isaac to God. The good patriarch rises early, and begins his sad journey. And now he travels three days, and Isaac still is in his sight! Misery is made worse when long continued. The expression, We will come again to you, shows that Abraham expected that Isaac, being raised from the dead, would return with him. It was a very affecting question that Isaac asked him, as they were going together: “My father,” said Isaac; it was a melting word, which, one would think, should strike deeper in the heart of Abraham, than his knife could in the heart of Isaac. Yet he waits for his son's question. Then Abraham, where he meant not, prophesies: “My son, God will provide a lamb for a burnt-offering.” The Holy Spirit, by his mouth, seems to predict the Lamb of God, which he has provided, and which taketh away the sin of the world. Abraham lays the wood in order for his Isaac's funeral pile, and now tells him the amazing news: Isaac, thou art the lamb which God has provided! Abraham, no doubt, comforting him with the same hopes with which he himself by faith was comforted. Yet it is necessary that the sacrifice be bound. The great Sacrifice, which, in the fulness of time, was to be offered up, must be bound, and so must Isaac. This being done, Abraham takes the knife, and stretches out his hand to give the fatal blow. Here is an act of faith and obedience, which deserves to be a spectacle to God, angels, and men. God, by his providence, calls us to part with an Isaac sometimes, and we must do it with cheerful submission to his holy will, 1Sa 3:18.
22.m:
Abraham faced a test stronger than any gold in a furnace. Most people would try to talk their way out of such a command, but Abraham trusted God completely. He knew God always keeps His promises and does what’s best.

Abraham didn’t delay. Early the next morning, he started on the painful trip. For three whole days, Isaac stayed close by his side. When troubles drag on, they feel even harder.

When they finally arrived at the place, Abraham told his servants, “We’ll come back later.” He believed God would raise Isaac from the dead. 

Isaac noticed his dad seemed sad and asked, “Father?” That simple question touched Abraham deeply. Still, he answered calmly, “God will provide a lamb.”

As they walked, Abraham prepared the wood for the offering. Then he told Isaac the shocking truth: “Son, you’re the lamb God chose!” Abraham kept his faith strong, just like God promised.

Next, he tied Isaac down. Just like Jesus, who would one day die for our sins, Isaac needed to be bound too. With knife in hand, Abraham got ready to kill his son.

This showed incredible trust and obedience. Sometimes God asks us to let go of things we love, and we need to follow His plan willingly, just like Abraham did.

22.o: It was not God's intention that Isaac should actually be sacrificed, yet nobler blood than that of animals, in due time, was to be shed for sin, even the blood of the only begotten Son of God. But in the mean while God would not in any case have human sacrifices used, another sacrifice is provided. Reference must be had to the promised Messiah, the blessed Seed. Christ was sacrificed in our stead, as this ram instead of Isaac, and his death was our discharge. And observe, that the temple, the place of sacrifice, was afterwards built upon this same mount Moriah; and Calvary, where Christ was crucified, was near. A new name was given to that place, for the encouragement of all believers, to the end of the world, cheerfully to trust in God, and obey him. Jehovah-jireh, the Lord will provide; probably alluding to what Abraham had said, God will provide himself a lamb. The Lord will always have his eye upon his people, in their straits and distresses, that he may give them seasonable help.
22.m:
God did not intend for Isaac to really be sacrificed. But one day, someone even more important than Isaac would die for sins—Jesus, God’s one and only son. For now, God wanted a different animal to take Isaac’s place. This points to Jesus, who came later to save us from our sins. Just like the ram took Isaac’s place, Jesus died in our place. 

The temple, where sacrifices were made, was built on Mount Moriah. And nearby was Golgotha, where Jesus was crucified. God gave a new name to that spot—to encourage everyone to trust and follow Him. “Jehovah-jireh” means “the Lord will provide.” It reminds us of what Abraham said, “God will provide a way.” God watches over His people and helps them when they need it most.

22.o: There are high declarations of God's favour to Abraham in this confirmation of the covenant with him, exceeding any he had yet been blessed with. Those that are willing to part with any thing for God, shall have it made up to them with unspeakable advantage. The promise, ver. #(18), doubtless points at the Messiah, and the grace of the gospel. Hereby we know the loving-kindness of God our Saviour towards sinful man, in that he hath not withheld his Son, his only Son, from us. Hereby we perceive the love of Christ, in that he gave himself a sacrifice for our sins. Yet he lives, and calls to sinners to come to him, and partake of his blood-bought salvation. He calls to his redeemed people to rejoice in him, and to glorify him. What then shall we render for all his benefits? Let his love constrain us to live not to ourselves, but to Him who died for us, and rose again. Admiring and adoring His grace, let us devote our all to his service, who laid down his life for our salvation. Whatever is dearest to us upon earth is our Isaac. And the only way for us to find comfort in an earthly thing, is to give it by faith into the hands of God. Yet remember that Abraham was not justified by his readiness to obey, but by the infinitely more noble obedience of Jesus Christ; his faith receiving this, relying on this, rejoicing in this, disposed and made him able for such wonderful self-denial and duty. (Ge 22:20-24)
22.m:
There are promises here showing God’s favor toward Abraham that were greater than anything he had received before. When someone gives something up for God, God will always replace it with even better things.

The promise in verse 18 surely refers to the Messiah and the blessings of the gospel. This shows God’s kindness to us—He did not hold back His one and only Son from us. It also shows how much Christ loves us because He gave Himself as a sacrifice for our sins. But He still lives and invites sinners to come to Him and share in His salvation. He tells those He has saved to be joyful and honor Him.

What should we do in response to all these gifts? We should use God’s love to motivate us to live for Him, not just for ourselves. Since He died for us and rose again, we should offer everything to serve Him. 

Whatever we treasure most on Earth is like our Isaac. The best way to find peace about what we value is to trustingly hand it over to God. However, remember that Abraham wasn’t declared right with God because of his willingness to obey, but because of the perfect obedience of Jesus Christ. Abraham’s faith accepted this, relied on it, and rejoiced in it. That’s what enabled him to show such amazing self-sacrifice and devotion.

22.o: This chapter ends with some account of Nahor's family, who had settled at Haran. This seems to be given for the connexion which it had with the church of God. From thence Isaac and Jacob took wives; and before the account of those events this list is recorded. It shows that though Abraham saw his own family highly honoured with privileges, admitted into covenant, and blessed with the assurance of the promise, yet he did not look with disdain upon his relations, but was glad to hear of the increase and welfare of their families.
22.m:
This chapter finishes by telling us about Nahor’s family, who lived in Haran. The information is important because it connects to God’s people. Isaac and Jacob found wives there later on. Before we get to those stories, we see this list of names. 

It shows that even though Abraham’s family got special blessings and promises from God, he still cared about his relatives. He was happy to know their families were growing well too.

23.o: The longest life must shortly come to a close. Blessed be God that there is a world where sin, death, vanity, and vexation cannot enter. Blessed be his name, that even death cannot part believers from union with Christ. Those whom we most love, yea, even our own bodies, which we so care for, must soon become loathsome lumps of clays, and be buried out of sight. How loose then should we be to all earthly attachments and adornments! Let us seek rather that our souls be adorned with heavenly graces. Abraham rendered honour and respect to the princes of Heth, although of the ungodly Canaanites. The religion of the Bible enjoins to pay due respect to all in authority, without flattering their persons, or countenancing their crimes if they are unworthy characters. And the noble generosity of these Canaanites shames and condemns the closeness, selfishness, and ill-humour of many that call themselves Israelites. It was not in pride that Abraham refused the gift, because he scorned to be beholden to Ephron; but in justice and in prudence. Abraham was able to pay for the field, and therefore would not take advantage of Ephron's generosity. Honesty, as well as honour, forbids us to take advantage of our neighbour's liberality, and to impose, upon those who give freely.
23.m:
The longest life will eventually end. Thank God there is a place where sin, death, vanity, and trouble don’t exist. Praise His name, because even death can't separate believers from Jesus. 

Those we love most, including our own bodies, will one day become repulsive piles of dirt and be hidden away. We should let go of all earthly things and focus on adorning our souls with godly qualities instead.

Abraham showed honor and respect to the leaders of Heth, even though they were ungodly Canaanites. The Bible teaches us to show proper respect to people in authority, without flattery or supporting their wrongdoings.

These Canaanites’ generous behavior puts to shame the greediness, selfishness, and bad attitudes of some who claim to be Israelites. When Abraham turned down the offer, it wasn’t out of pride. He didn’t want to owe anything to Ephron. Instead, it was about fairness and wisdom. Abraham could afford to buy the land, so he wouldn’t take unfair advantage of Ephron’s kindness. 

Honesty and good manners tell us not to exploit others' generosity by forcing them to do more than they offered freely.

23.o: Prudence, as well as justice, directs us to be fair and open in our dealings; cheating bargains will not bear the light. Abraham, without fraud or delay, pays the money. He pays it at once in full, without keeping any part back; and by weight, current money with the merchant, without deceit. See how anciently money was used for the help of trade, and how honestly it should be paid when it is due. Though all the land of Canaan was Abraham by promise, yet the time of his possessing it not being come, what he had occasion for he bought and paid for. Dominion is not founded in grace. The saints' title to an eternal inheritance does not entitle them to the possessions of this world, nor justify them in doing wrong. Ephron honestly and fairly makes a good title to the land. As that which is bought, must be honestly paid for, so that which is sold, must be honestly delivered and secured. Let us manage our concerns with punctuality and exactness, in order to avoid contention. Abraham buried Sarah in cave. or vault, which was in the purchased field. It would tend to endear the land to his posterity. And it is worth noting, that a burying-place was the only piece of the land which Abraham possessed in Canaan. Those who have least of this earth, find a grave in it. This sepulchre was at the end of the field; whatever our possessions are, there is a burial-place at the end of them. It was a token of his belief and expectation of the resurrection. Abraham is contented to be still a pilgrim while he lives, but secures a place where, when he dies, his flesh may rest in hope. After all, the chief concern is, with whom we shall rise.
23.m:
Being honest and straightforward in business is wise. Cheating deals won't stand up to scrutiny. 

Abraham paid the money right away, without any tricks. He gave the full amount, using proper weights and measures, just like merchants do. Money has been around since ancient times to help people buy and sell things honestly.

Even though God promised Abraham all of Canaan, he still needed to pay for what he wanted now. Having faith doesn’t mean you don’t need to follow normal rules about buying and selling. 

Ephron acted fairly too, making sure Abraham got clear ownership of the land. When you buy something, pay for it properly. When you sell, give it over clearly. Handle your affairs carefully to avoid arguments.

Abraham buried Sarah in a cave within the field he bought. Owning even a small plot showed his connection to the land. A gravesite was the only part of Canaan Abraham owned during his life. Even those with little on Earth get a final resting place here.

This tomb marked Abraham’s belief in the future resurrection. While alive, he remained a stranger in the land, but he made sure to secure a spot where his body could lie in peace until then. In the end, the most important thing is whose side we’ll be on when we rise again.

24.o: The effect of good example, good teaching, and the worship of God in a family, will generally appear in the piety, faithfulness, prudence, and affection of the servants. To live in such families, or to have such servants, both are blessings from God which should be highly valued, and thankfully acknowledged. But no concern in life is of greater importance to ourselves, to others, or to the church of God, than marriage. It therefore ought always to be undertaken with much care and prudence, especially with reference to the will of God, and with prayer for his direction and blessing. Where good parents are not consulted and regarded, the blessing of God cannot be expected. Parents, in disposing of their children, should carefully consult the welfare of their souls, and their furtherance in the way to heaven. Observe the charge Abraham gave to a good servant, one whose conduct, faithfulness, and affection, to him and his family, he had long known. Observe also, that Abraham remembers that God had wonderfully brought him out of the land of his birth, by the call of his grace; and therefore doubts not but He will prosper his care, not to bring his son thither again. God will cause that to end in our comfort, in which we sincerely aim at his glory.
24.m:
Good examples, good teaching, and regular worship in a family usually show up in the kindness, honesty, wisdom, and love of the servants. Living in such homes or having these kinds of servants are blessings from God that we should treasure and thank Him for.

But nothing in life is more important to us, to others, or to the church than getting married. So people should take great care and thought when choosing a spouse, especially considering what God wants and praying for His guidance.

If good parents aren't involved and respected, you can’t expect God’s blessing on the marriage. When parents choose partners for their children, they need to think about the child’s soul and helping them get closer to heaven.

Notice how Abraham instructed his trusted servant who was faithful and loving towards him and his family. Abraham remembered how God had led him away from his homeland through His calling, so he believed God would help him find a good wife for Isaac.

God will bless our efforts if we truly seek to honor Him.

24.o: Abraham's servant devoutly acknowledged God. We have leave to be particular in recommending our affairs to the care of Divine providence. He proposes a sign, not that he intended to proceed no further, if not gratified in it; but it is a prayer that God would provide a good wife for his young master; and that was a good prayer. She should be simple, industrious, humble, cheerful, serviceable, and hospitable. Whatever may be the fashion, common sense, as well as piety, tells us, these are the proper qualifications for a wife and mother; for one who is to be a companion to her husband, the manager of domestic concerns, and trusted to form the minds of children. When the steward came to seek a wife for his master, he did not go to places of amusement and sinful pleasure, and pray that he might meet one there, but to the well of water, expecting to find one there employed aright. He prayed that God would please to make his way in this matter plain and clear before him. Our times are in God's hand; not only events themselves, but the times of them. We must take heed of being over-bold in urging what God should do, lest the event should weaken our faith, rather than strengthen it. But God owned him by making his way clear. Rebekah, in all respects, answered the characters he sought for in the woman that was to be his master's wife. When she came to the well, she went down and filled her pitcher, and came up to go home with it. She did not stand to gaze upon the strange man his camels, but minded her business, and would not have been diverted from it but by an opportunity of doing good. She did not curiously or confidently enter into discourse with him, but answered him modestly. Being satisfied that the Lord had heard his prayer, he gave the damsel some ornaments worn in eastern countries; asking at the same time respecting her kindred. On learning that she was of his master's relations, he bowed down his head and worshipped, blessing God. His words were addressed to the Lord, but being spoken in the hearing of Rebekah, she could perceive who he was, and whence he came.
24.m:
Abraham's servant prayed sincerely to God. It’s okay to ask God to help with our personal matters. The servant asked for a sign, not because he wouldn’t continue without it, but because he wanted God to choose a good wife for Isaac. This was a good prayer. The wife should be hardworking, humble, happy, helpful, and kind. Common sense and faith tell us these are important qualities for a wife and mother. 

When the servant looked for a wife for his master, he didn't go to fun places where people sin. Instead, he went to the well to look for someone working there. He asked God to show him clearly which girl would be right for Isaac. God controls everything, including timing. We shouldn’t push God too much about what should happen, because it might shake our faith. But God helped by showing the servant the right path.

Rebekah matched all the qualities the servant hoped for in Isaac’s future wife. At the well, she fetched water and carried it back home. She wasn’t distracted by the stranger or his camels but focused on her work. She didn’t chat too much, just answered politely. Seeing God had answered his prayers, the servant gave her gifts. Then he asked about her family. Learning she was related to Abraham, he bowed and thanked God. Everyone nearby, especially Rebekah, knew who he was and where he came from.

24.o: The making up of the marriage between Isaac and Rebekah is told very particularly. We are to notice God's providence in the common events of human life, and in them to exercise prudence and other graces. Laban went to ask Abraham's servant in, but not till he saw the ear-ring, and bracelet upon his sister's hands. We know Laban's character, by his conduct afterwards, and may think that he would not have been so free to entertain him, if he had not hoped to be well rewarded for it. The servant was intent upon his business. Though he was come off a journey, and come to a good house, he would not eat till he had told his errand. The doing our work, and the fulfilling our trusts, either for God or man, should be preferred by us before our food: it was our Saviour's meat and drink, Joh 4:34. He tells them the charge his master had given him, with the reason of it. He relates what had happened at the well, to further the proposal, plainly showing the finger of God in it. Those events which to us seem the effect of choice, contrivance, or chance, are “appointed out” of God. This hinders not, but rather encourages the use of all proper means. They freely and cheerfully close with the proposal; and any matter is likely to be comfortable, when it proceeds from the Lord. Abraham's servant thankfully acknowledges the good success he had met with. He was a humble man, and humble men are not ashamed to own their situation in life, whatever it may be. All our temporal concerns are sweet if intermixed with godliness.
24.m:
The story of Isaac and Rebekah’s marriage is described in detail. We should pay attention to God’s guidance in daily life and show wisdom and virtue. 

Laban invited the servant inside only after seeing the ring on his sister’s hand and the bracelets. His later actions reveal Laban’s nature, suggesting he might not have welcomed the stranger otherwise unless expecting some reward.

The servant focused solely on his mission. Even though he arrived tired and at a nice home, he refused to eat until he shared his purpose. Doing our tasks and fulfilling our duties for God or others should take priority over eating. Jesus said this about his own work, John 4:34.

He explained the task his master gave him and the reasons behind it. Then he recounted the events at the well that helped make the match, clearly pointing to God’s involvement. Things that seem random or planned often happen because God directs them.

This doesn’t stop us from using normal methods to achieve goals. Everyone agreed happily with the plan since it came from God. The servant thanked God for helping him succeed. He was modest and didn’t mind sharing his status. Our worldly affairs become joyful when mixed with faithfulness to God.

24.o: Abraham's servant, as one that chose his work before his pleasure, was for hastening home. Lingering and loitering no way become a wise and good man who is faithful to his duty. As children ought not to marry without their parents' consent, so parents ought not to marry them without their own. Rebekah consented, not only to go, but to go at once. The goodness of Rebekah's character shows there was nothing wrong in her answer, though it be not agreeable to modern customs among us. We may hope that she had such an idea of the religion and godliness in the family she was to go to, as made her willing to forget her own people and her father's house. Her friends dismiss her with suitable attendants, and with hearty good wishes. They blessed Rebekah. When our relations are entering into a new condition, we ought by prayer to commend them to the blessing and grace of God. Isaac was well employed when he met Rebekah. He went out to take the advantage of a silent evening, and a solitary place, for meditation and prayer; those divine exercises by which we converse with God and our own hearts. Holy souls love retirement; it will do us good to be often alone, if rightly employed; and we are never less alone than when alone. Observe what an affectionate son Isaac was: it was about three years since his mother died, and yet he was not, till now, comforted. See also what an affectionate husband he was to his wife. Dutiful sons promise fair to be affectionate husbands; he that fills up his first station in life with honour, is likely to do the same in those that follow.
24.m:
Abraham’s servant wanted to hurry back home because he valued his duties over his personal desires. A smart and kind person should always be eager to fulfill their responsibilities.

Just as children shouldn’t get married without their parents’ approval, parents shouldn’t force marriage on their children either.

Rebekah agreed not just to leave, but to leave right away. Since Rebekah was a good person, we know her decision wasn't wrong even if it doesn’t fit today's customs.

We can assume she felt happy about joining a religious and pious family, making her willing to leave behind her old life.

Her family sent her off with proper company and sincere blessings. 

When family members start a new chapter, we should pray for them and ask God to bless them.

Isaac was busy praying when he met Rebekah. He would walk outside during quiet evenings to meditate and talk to God. People who love God enjoy being alone to think and pray.

Notice how loving Isaac was toward Rebekah. His mom had passed away around three years ago, but he hadn’t found comfort until meeting Rebekah. This shows Isaac cared deeply for his future wife too.

Someone who respects their roles in life usually treats others kindly as well.

25.o: All the days, even of the best and greatest saints, are not remarkable days; some slide on silently; such were these last days of Abraham. Here is an account of Abraham's children by Keturah, and the disposition which he made of his estate. After the birth of these sons, he set his house in order, with prudence and justice. He did this while he yet lived. It is wisdom for men to do what they find to do while they live, as far as they can. Abraham lived 175 years; just one hundred years after he came to Canaan; so long he was a sojourner in a strange country. Whether our stay in this life be long or short, it matters but little, provided we leave behind us a testimony to the faithfulness and goodness of the Lord, and a good example to our families. We are told that his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him. It seems that Abraham had himself brought them together while he lived. Let us not close the history of the life of Abraham without blessing God for such a testimony of the triumph of faith.
25.m:
All the days of even the best and greatest saints are not always special days; sometimes their lives go quietly day by day. Such were the final days of Abraham. This section tells about Abraham’s children from Keturah and how he divided up his property. 

After having those sons, he organized his household wisely and fairly. He did this while he was still alive. It’s smart for people to take care of things they need to do while they’re still around, as much as possible. 

Abraham lived for 175 years, exactly 100 years after arriving in Canaan. So long was he a stranger in a foreign land. Whether our time here is long or short, it doesn’t really matter as long as we leave behind proof of God’s faithfulness and kindness, and a good example for our family. 

We learn that Isaac and Ishmael buried him. It looks like Abraham got them both together while he was still living. As we finish the story of Abraham’s life, let’s thank God for the victory of faith shown through him.

25.o: Ishmael had twelve sons, whose families became distinct tribes. They peopled a very large country that lay between Egypt and Assyria, called Arabia. The number and strength of this family were the fruit of the promise, made to Hagar and to Abraham, concerning Ishmael.
