25.m: Ishmael had twelve sons who each started their own tribe. These tribes lived in a big area between Egypt and Assyria known as Arabia. The size and strength of Ishmael’s family came from promises God made to both Hagar and Abraham about Ishmael.

25.o: Isaac seems not to have been much tried, but to have spent his days in quietness. Jacob and Esau were prayed for; their parents, after being long childless, obtained them by prayer. The fulfilment of God's promise is always sure, yet it is often slow. The faith of believers is tried, their patience exercised, and mercies long waited for are more welcome when they come. Isaac and Rebekah kept in view the promise of all nations being blessed in their posterity, therefore were not only desirous of children, but anxious concerning every thing which seemed to mark their future character. In all our doubts we should inquire of the Lord by prayer. In many of our conflicts with sin and temptation, we may adopt Rebekah's words, “If it be so, why am I thus?” If a child of God, why so careless or carnal? If not a child of God, why so afraid of, or so burdened with sin?
25.m:
Isaac lived a peaceful life without much trouble. Jacob and Esau were both prayed for. Their parents had been unable to have children for a long time, but they received them through prayer. 

God’s promises always come true, even if they take a while. This tests the faith of believers and exercises their patience. When mercies finally arrive after waiting, they are especially sweet.

Isaac and Rebekah remembered God’s promise that all nations would be blessed through their descendants. So they wanted children and cared deeply about everything that might affect their future character. 

When we’re unsure, we should ask God through prayer. Sometimes, when struggling with sin or temptation, we might wonder, just like Rebekah did: "If this is true, then why do I feel this way?" If you are a child of God, why are you so careless or worldly? And if you aren’t a child of God, why are you so troubled by or weighed down by sin?

25.o: Esau hunted the beasts of the field with dexterity and success, till he became a conqueror, ruling over his neighbours. Jacob was a plain man, one that liked the true delights of retirement, better than all pretended pleasures. He was a stranger and a pilgrim in his spirit, and a shepherd all his days. Isaac and Rebekah had but these two children, one was the father's darling, and the other the mother's. And though godly parents must feel their affections most drawn over towards a godly child, yet they will not show partiality. Let their affections lead them to do what is just and equal to every child, or evils will arise.
25.m:
Esau was good at hunting animals and became successful, ruling over his neighbors. Jacob preferred quiet activities and was more interested in being a shepherd. He lived as if he were a stranger in a foreign land. 

Isaac and Rebekah only had two children—Esau and Jacob. One was the favorite of the father, while the other was the favorite of the mother. Even though godly parents may naturally love a godly child more, they should still treat each child fairly. Otherwise, problems might happen.

25.o: We have here the bargain made between Jacob and Esau about the right, which was Esau's by birth, but Jacob's by promise. It was for a spiritual privilege; and we see Jacob's desire of the birth-right, but he sought to obtain it by crooked courses, not like his character as a plain man. He was right, that he coveted earnestly the best gifts; he was wrong, that he took advantage of his brother's need. The inheritance of their father's worldly goods did not descend to Jacob, and was not meant in this proposal. But it includeth the future possession of the land of Canaan by his children's children, and the covenant made with Abraham as to Christ the promised Seed. Believing Jacob valued these above all things; unbelieving Esau despised them. Yet although we must be of Jacob's judgment in seeking the birth-right, we ought carefully to avoid all guile, in seeking to obtain even the greatest advantages. Jacob's pottage pleased Esau's eye. “Give me some of that red;” for this he was called Edom, or Red. Gratifying the sensual appetite ruins thousands of precious souls. When men's hearts walk after their own eyes, Job 31:7, and when they serve their own bellies, they are sure to be punished. If we use ourselves to deny ourselves, we break the force of most temptations. It cannot be supposed that Esau was dying of hunger in Isaac's house. The words signify, I am going towards death; he seems to mean, I shall never live to inherit Canaan, or any of those future supposed blessings; and what signifies it who has them when I am dead and gone. This would be the language of profaneness, with which the apostle brands him, Heb 12:16; and this contempt of the birth-right is blamed, ver. #(34). It is the greatest folly to part with our interest in God, and Christ, and heaven, for the riches, honours, and pleasures of this world; it is as bad a bargain as his who sold a birth-right for a dish of pottage. Esau ate and drank, pleased his palate, satisfied his appetite, and then carelessly rose up and went his way, without any serious thought, or any regret, about the bad bargain he had made. Thus Esau despised his birth-right. By his neglect and contempt afterwards, and by justifying himself in what he had done, he put the bargain past recall. People are ruined, not so much by doing what is amiss, as by doing it and not repenting of it.
25.m:
Here we see Jacob making a deal with Esau about the birthright, which belonged to Esau by birth but Jacob got through a promise. Jacob wanted a spiritual blessing, but he tried to get it using sneaky ways, which wasn’t fitting for someone known as honest. 

Jacob’s wish for the birthright was good, but taking advantage of his brother’s situation was wrong. The agreement didn't include their father’s material possessions. Instead, it covered the future ownership of the land of Canaan by Jacob’s descendants and the promises given to Abraham about Christ. Because Jacob believed, he saw these blessings as more valuable than anything else. Unbeliever Esau, however, looked down on them. While we should seek the birthright like Jacob, we must always be careful not to trick others to get it.

Esau liked Jacob’s stew because it looked tasty. That’s why he asked for some of the red stuff, earning him the nickname Edom, or Red. Pleasing the senses often leads many people astray. When people follow their desires and appetites, they end up being punished. If we learn to resist temptation, we weaken its power over us.

It’s unlikely Esau was starving at home. His words suggest he wouldn’t live long enough to inherit Canaan or other future blessings anyway. Such talk shows a lack of respect for sacred things, as the apostle points out in Hebrews 12:16. Selling the birthright for food showed great foolishness—giving up eternal rewards for temporary pleasures. After eating and drinking, Esau left without thinking twice about his poor decision. This disregard for the birthright led to his downfall.

By ignoring it later and saying it was okay, he made it impossible to undo the deal. People get hurt more by doing wrong things and then not feeling sorry about them than by doing those things alone.

26.o: Isaac had been trained up in a believing dependence upon the Divine grant of the land of Canaan to him and his heirs; and now that there is a famine in the land, Isaac still cleaves to the covenant. The real worth of God's promises cannot be lessened to a believer by any cross providences that may befall him. If God engage to be with us, and we are where he would have us to be, nothing but our own unbelief and distrust can prevent our comfort. The obedience of Abraham to the Divine command, was evidence of that faith, whereby, as a sinner, he was justified before God, and the effect of that love whereby true faith works. God testifies that he approved this obedience, to encourage others, especially Isaac.
26.m:
Isaac grew up learning to trust God’s promise about giving him and his family the land of Canaan. Even during a time of drought, Isaac held onto that promise. 

God’s promises don’t lose their value even if bad things happen. If God says He will be with you, and you’re where He wants you to be, only doubt can stop you from finding joy. 

Abraham obeyed God’s commands because of his faith. This showed that he trusted God enough to be seen as righteous. It came from the love that true faith creates. 

When God praised Abraham for following His commands, He wanted to inspire others—especially Isaac—to do the same.

26.o: There is nothing in Isaac's denial of his wife to be imitated, nor even excused. The temptation of Isaac is the same as that which overcame his father, and that in two instances. This rendered his conduct the greater sin. The falls of those who are gone before us are so many rocks on which others have split; and the recording of them is like placing buoys to save future mariners. This Abimelech was not the same that lived in Abraham's days, but both acted rightly. The sins of professors shame them before those that are not themselves religious.
26.m:
Isaac shouldn't have denied that Rebekah was his wife. His excuse for doing so wasn’t good enough. Like his dad, he gave in to the same temptation twice. That made his mistake worse. When people who follow God mess up, their mistakes warn others to avoid similar problems. 

Abimelech wasn’t the same king who had dealt with Abraham. But both kings did what was right. When followers of God do wrong things, it embarrasses them in front of people who aren’t religious.

26.o: God blessed Isaac. Be it observed, for the encouragement of poor tenants who occupy other people's lands, and are honest and industrious, that God blessed him with a great increase. The Philistines envied Isaac. It is an instance of the vanity of the world; for the more men have of it, the more they are envied, and exposed to censure and injury. Also of the corruption of nature; for that is an ill principle indeed, which makes men grieve at the good of others. They made Isaac go out of their country. That wisdom which is from above, will teach us to give up our right, and to draw back from contentions. If we are wrongfully driven from one place, the Lord will make room for us in another.
26.m:
God blessed Isaac. This shows that even if you're just renting land and working hard, God can bless you with plenty.

The Philistines were jealous of Isaac because he was doing well. This teaches us that the more success someone has, the more they might be resented by others. People often feel bad about others' good fortune.

Because the Philistines were envious, they forced Isaac to leave their area. But true wisdom tells us to let go of what isn't ours and avoid arguments. When we’re unfairly pushed away from somewhere, God will find new places for us.

26.o: Isaac met with much opposition in digging wells. Two were called Contention and Hatred. See the nature of worldly things; they make quarrels, and are occasions of strife; and what is often the lot of the most quiet and peaceable; those who avoid striving, yet cannot avoid being striven with. And what a mercy it is to have plenty of water; to have it without striving for it! The more common this mercy is, the more reason to be thankful for it. At length Isaac digged a well, for which they strove not. Those that study to be quiet, seldom fail of being so. When men are false and unkind, still God is faithful and gracious; and his time to show himself so is, when we are most disappointed by men. The same night that Isaac came weary and uneasy to Beer-sheba, God brought comforts to his soul. Those may remove with comfort who are sure of God's presence.
26.m:
Isaac faced many problems while digging wells. Two of them were named Quarrel and Conflict. This shows how worldly things can cause arguments and fights. Even peaceful people sometimes get involved in disputes.

Having plenty of water without fighting over it is a blessing. We should be grateful for such blessings, even if they are common. 

Finally, Isaac found a well where there was no conflict. People who try to stay out of trouble usually succeed. 

When others are dishonest and mean, God remains true and kind. He helps us when we need him most. On the very night Isaac felt tired and troubled at Beer-sheba, God gave him comfort. People can move with peace knowing God will always be with them.

26.o: When a man's ways please the Lord, he maketh even his enemies to be at peace with him, Pr 16:7. Kings' hearts are in his hands, and when he pleases, he can turn them to favour his people. It is not wrong to stand upon our guard in dealing with those who have acted unfairly. But Isaac did not insist on the unkindnesses they had done him; he freely entered into friendship with them. Religion teaches us to be neighbourly, and, as much as in us lies, to live peaceable with all men. Providence smiled upon what Isaac did; God blessed his labours.
26.m:
When a person's actions please the Lord, He makes their enemies become friendly toward them, Proverbs 16:7. The king's heart is in God’s control, and if He wants to, He can get kings to favor His people. It is okay to protect ourselves from unfair treatment. But Isaac didn't focus on the mean things others had done to him. Instead, he made friends with them. 

Religion teaches us to be good neighbors and try to live peacefully with everyone. God was pleased with what Isaac did. He blessed Isaac's work.

26.o: Esau was foolish in marrying two wives together, and still more in marrying Canaanites, strangers to the blessing of Abraham, and subject to the curse of Noah. It grieved his parents that he married without their advice and consent. It grieved them that he married among those who had no religion. Children have little reason to expect God's blessing who do that which is a grief of mind to good parents.
26.m: Esau made a big mistake by marrying two women at once, and an even bigger one by choosing wives from the Canaanite people. These were people who weren’t part of Abraham’s blessings and were under Noah’s curse. His parents were upset because he got married without asking for their permission first. They were also sad because he chose partners who didn’t believe in God. When children do things that hurt their parents’ feelings, they shouldn’t be surprised if God doesn’t bless them.

27.o: The promises of the Messiah, and of the land of Canaan, had come down to Isaac. Isaac being now about 135 years of age, and his sons about 75, and not duly considering the Divine word concerning his two sons, that the elder should serve the younger, resolved to put all the honour and power that were in the promise, upon Esau his eldest son. We are very apt to take measures rather from our own reason than from Divine revelation, and thereby often miss our way.
27.m:
The promises about the Messiah and the land of Canaan came down to Isaac. Now, Isaac was around 135 years old, and his sons were about 75. He did not think carefully about God’s words saying that the younger son would be more important. So, he decided to give all the honor and power promised by God to Esau, his oldest son.

We tend to make decisions based on our own ideas instead of what God tells us. This often leads us to go wrong.

27.o: Rebekah knew that the blessing was intended for Jacob, and expected he would have it. But she wronged Isaac by putting a cheat on him; she wronged Jacob by tempting him to wickedness. She put a stumbling-block in Esau's way, and gave him a pretext for hatred to Jacob and to religion. All were to be blamed. It was one of those crooked measures often adopted to further the Divine promises; as if the end would justify, or excuse wrong means. Thus many have acted wrong, under the idea of being useful in promoting the cause of Christ. The answer to all such things is that which God addressed to Abraham, I am God Almighty; walk before me and be thou perfect. And it was a very rash speech of Rebekah, “Upon me be thy curse, my son.” Christ has borne the curse of the law for all who take upon them the yoke of the command, the command of the gospel. But it is too daring for any creature to say, Upon me be thy curse.
27.m:
Rebekah knew the blessing was meant for Jacob and thought he should get it. But she did wrong by tricking Isaac. She tempted Jacob to do bad things. This made life harder for Esau and gave him reason to hate Jacob and religion. Everyone involved was at fault.

This was an unfair way to help God’s promises come true. Some people think doing wrong is okay if it helps promote Jesus’ work. But God told Abraham, "I am the almighty God. Walk with me and be perfect." 

It was risky for Rebekah to say, "Put a curse on me instead, my son." Only Jesus took the punishment for following God’s commands. No person should ever say, "Put a curse on me."

27.o: Jacob, with some difficulty, gained his point, and got the blessing. This blessing is in very general terms. No mention is made of the distinguishing mercies in the covenant with Abraham. This might be owing to Isaac having Esau in his mind, though it was Jacob who was before him. He could not be ignorant how Esau had despised the best things. Moreover, his attachment to Esau, so as to disregard the mind of God, must have greatly weakened his own faith in these things. It might therefore be expected, that leanness would attend his blessing, agreeing with the state of his mind.
27.m:
Jacob finally got the blessing, even though it wasn't easy for him. The blessing was very general. It did not include the special promises given to Abraham. 

This may have been because Isaac thought about Esau instead of Jacob. Isaac knew that Esau had rejected the most important things. Also, Isaac favored Esau so much that he ignored what God wanted. This likely weakened Isaac’s faith.

So, it makes sense that the blessing was weak, matching Isaac’s state of mind.

27.o: When Esau understood that Jacob had got the blessing, he cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry. The day is coming, when those that now make light of the blessings of the covenant, and sell their title to spiritual blessings for that which is of no value, will, in vain, ask urgently for them. Isaac, when made sensible of the deceit practised on him, trembled exceedingly. Those who follow the choice of their own affections, rather than the Divine will, get themselves into perplexity. But he soon recovers, and confirms the blessing he had given to Jacob, saying, I have blessed him, and he shall be blessed. Those who part with their wisdom and grace, their faith and a good conscience, for the honours, wealth, or pleasures of this world, however they feign a zeal for the blessing, have judged themselves unworthy of it, and their doom shall be accordingly. A common blessing was bestowed upon Esau. This he desired. Faint desires of happiness, without right choice of the end, and right use of the means, deceive many unto their own ruin. Multitudes go to hell with their mouths full of good wishes. The great difference is, that there is nothing in Esau's blessing which points at Christ; and without that, the fatness of the earth, and the plunder of the field, will stand in little stead. Thus Isaac, by faith, blessed both his sons, according as their lot should be.
27.m:
When Esau found out that Jacob had gotten the blessing, he cried loudly and bitterly. One day, people who now ignore God’s promises and trade their chance at true blessings for worthless things will beg for those blessings and find it too late.

Isaac was shocked when he realized he’d been tricked. People who choose what feels good over God’s plan often cause trouble for themselves. But Isaac quickly calmed down and reaffirmed the blessing he gave to Jacob. He said, “I’ve already blessed him, and he’ll keep being blessed.”

Those who give up their wisdom, faith, and good conscience for worldly honors, money, or pleasure show they don’t really want God’s best. They lose their chance at real blessings.

Esau received a general blessing. That’s what he asked for. Weak hopes for happiness without choosing the right goals and using the right methods fool many people. Many head to hell while wishing for better outcomes.

The main difference is that Esau’s blessing didn’t include Jesus. Without Jesus, having lots of food and land won’t help much. So Isaac, through faith, blessed both his sons based on their paths in life.

27.o: Esau bore malice to Jacob on account of the blessing he had obtained. Thus he went in the way of Cain, who slew his brother, because he gained that acceptance with God of which he had rendered himself unworthy. Esau aimed to prevent Jacob or his seed from having the dominion, by taking away his life. Men may fret at God's counsels, but cannot change them. To prevent mischief, Rebekah warned Jacob of his danger, and advised him to withdraw for his safety. We must not presume too far upon the wisdom and resolution, even of the most hopeful and promising children; but care must be taken to keep them out of the way of evil. When reading this chapter, we should not fail to observe, that we must not follow even the best of men further than they act according to the law of God. We must not do evil that good may come. And though God overruled the bad actions recorded in this chapter, to fulfil his purposes, yet we see his judgment of them, in the painful consequences to all the parties concerned. It was the peculiar privilege and advantage of Jacob to convey these spiritual blessings to all nations. The Christ, the Saviour of the world, was to be born of some one family; and Jacob's was preferred to Esau's, out of the good pleasure of Almighty God, who is certainly the best judge of what is fit, and has an undoubted right to dispense his favours as he sees proper, Ro 9:12–15.
27.m:
Esau was angry with Jacob because Jacob got the blessing. He acted like Cain, who killed his brother because Cain’s offering wasn’t accepted by God. Esau wanted to stop Jacob or his descendants from ruling by killing Jacob. People might get upset about God’s plans, but they can't change them.

Rebekah told Jacob about the danger and suggested he leave for his safety. We shouldn’t assume our kids will always make wise choices. We need to protect them from trouble. 

When we read this story, remember not to copy even the best people if they go against God’s laws. Doing wrong to try to achieve something good isn’t okay. Even though God used the events here to fulfill His plans, we see the pain it caused everyone involved.

Jacob had a special role to pass on spiritual blessings to all nations. Jesus, the Savior of the world, would come from Jacob’s line, chosen by God’s grace. God knows what’s best and has the right to choose whom He blesses, as seen in Romans 9:12–15.

28.o: Jacob had blessings promised both as to this world and that which is to come; yet goes out to a hard service. This corrected him for the fraud on his father. The blessing shall be conferred on him, yet he shall smart for the indirect course taken to obtain it. Jacob is dismissed by his father with a solemn charge. He must not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan: those who profess religion, should not marry with those that care not for religion. Also with a solemn blessing. Isaac had before blessed him unwittingly; now he does it designedly. This blessing is more full than the former; it is a gospel blessing. This promise looks as high as heaven, of which Canaan was a type. That was the better country which Jacob and the other patriarchs had in view.
28.m:
Jacob received promises of blessings for this life and the next. But he went off to do difficult work. This helped correct him for tricking his father. 

The blessing would still be given to him, but he would face consequences for his dishonest actions.

Before sending Jacob away, his father gave him a serious warning. He told Jacob not to marry a woman from Canaan because she might not share his faith. 

Isaac also gave Jacob a special blessing. At first, he did so unknowingly, but now he did it on purpose. This new blessing was even fuller than the last one. It was like a promise from the Gospel.

This promise pointed to an even greater reward in heaven, which Canaan represented. The best place Jacob and others looked forward to was heaven.

28.o: Good examples impress even the profane and malicious. But Esau thought, by pleasing his parents in one thing, to atone for other wrong doings. Carnal hearts are apt to think themselves as good as they should be, because in some one matter they are not so bad as they have been.
28.m: Good examples can influence even those who are not religious or mean-spirited. But Esau believed that if he pleased his parents in one way, it would make up for his other wrongdoings. People with selfish hearts often think they are doing well enough because they are not as bad in one area.

28.o: Jacob's conduct hitherto, as recorded, was not that of one who simply feared and trusted in God. But now in trouble, obliged to flee, he looked only to God to make him to dwell in safety, and he could lie down and sleep in the open air with his head upon a stone. Any true believer would be willing to take up with Jacob's pillow, provided he might have Jacob's vision. God's time to visit his people with his comforts, is, when they are most destitute of other comforts, and other comforters. Jacob saw a ladder which reached from earth to heaven, the angels going up and coming down, and God himself at the head of it. This represents, 1. The providence of God, by which there is a constant intercourse kept up between heaven and earth. This let Jacob know that he had both a good guide and a good guard. 2. The mediation of Christ. He is this ladder; the foot on earth in his human nature, the top in heaven in his Divine nature. Christ is the Way; all God's favours come to us, and all our services go to him, by Christ, Joh 1:51. By this way, sinners draw near to the throne of grace with acceptance. By faith we perceive this way, and in prayer we approach by it. In answer to prayer we receive all needful blessings of providence and grace. We have no way of getting to heaven but by Christ. And when the soul, by faith, can see these things, then every place will become pleasant, and every prospect joyful. He will never leave us, until his last promise is accomplished in our everlasting happiness. God now spake comfortably to Jacob. He spake from the head of the ladder. All the glad tidings we receive from heaven come through Jesus Christ. The Messiah should come from Jacob. Christ is the great blessing of the world. All that are blessed, are blessed in him, and none of any family are shut out from blessedness in him, but those that shut out themselves. Jacob had to fear danger from his brother Esau; but God promises to keep him. He had a long journey before him; to an unknown country; but, Behold, I am with thee, and God promises to bring him back again to this land. He seemed to be forsaken of all his friends; but God gives him this assurance, I will not leave thee. Whom God loves, he never leaves.
28.m:
Jacob’s actions so far were not those of someone who truly feared and trusted God. But now, in trouble and forced to run away, he turned to God for protection and peace. He slept outdoors with his head resting on a rock. A real believer would gladly use Jacob’s simple pillow if it meant having Jacob’s dream.

God often visits His people with comfort when they lack everything else. Jacob dreamed about a ladder reaching from Earth to Heaven. Angels went up and down the ladder, while God stood above it. 

This dream showed:
1. God’s care over us, keeping a steady connection between Heaven and Earth.
2. How Christ acts as a bridge. One end touches Earth in His human form, the other reaches Heaven in His divine nature. Through Him, all God’s kindness comes to us, and all our prayers reach Him. Sinners get close to God’s mercy through Christ. Faith lets us see this path, and prayer helps us follow it. When we pray, we receive what we need.

Without Christ, we cannot reach Heaven. Once we trust in Him, everywhere feels peaceful and hopeful. God promised to stay with Jacob until his final promise came true—his eternal joy.

Now, God spoke kindly to Jacob. He said it from the top of the ladder, showing that all good news comes through Jesus. The Messiah would come from Jacob’s line. Everyone who finds blessing finds it in Christ. None are left out except those who exclude themselves.

Jacob worried about meeting his angry brother Esau. But God promised to protect him. Jacob faced a long trip to a new land, but God said He would return Jacob safely home.

He felt abandoned by all his friends, but God promised, "I will not leave you." God never abandons those He loves.

28.o: God manifested himself and his favour, to Jacob, when he was asleep. The Spirit, like the wind, blows when and where it listeth, and God's grace, like the dew, tarrieth not for the sons of men. Jacob sought to improve the visit God had made him. Wherever we are, in the city or in the desert, in the house or in the field, in the shop or in the street, we may keep up our intercourse with Heaven, if it is not our own fault. But the more we see of God, the more cause we see for holy trembling before him.
28.m:
God showed himself and his favor to Jacob while Jacob was sleeping. The Spirit moves freely, just like the wind. And God’s grace comes at any time, like morning dew. 

Jacob wanted to use the time God spent with him. We can stay connected to heaven wherever we are—whether in town or out in nature, whether inside or outside, whether at work or on the street—as long as we don’t stop ourselves from doing so.

The more we experience God, the more we feel a need to be humble and scared of Him in a good way.

28.o: Jacob made a solemn vow on this occasion. In this observe, 1. Jacob's faith. He trusts that God will be with him, and will keep him; he depends upon it. 2. Jacob's moderation in his desires. He asks not for soft clothing and dainty meat. If God give us much, we are bound to be thankful, and to use it for him; if he gives us but little, we are bound to be content, and cheerfully to enjoy him in it. 3. Jacob's piety, and his regard to God, appear in what he desired, that God would be with him, and keep him. We need desire no more to make us easy and happy. Also his resolution is, to cleave to the Lord, as his God in covenant. When we receive more than common mercy from God, we should abound in gratitude to him. The tenth is a fit proportion to be devoted to God, and employed for him; though it may be more or less, as God prospers us, 1Co 16:2. Let us then remember our Bethels, how we stand engaged by solemn vows to yield ourselves to the Lord, to take him for our God, and to devote all we have and are to his glory!
28.m:
Jacob made a solemn vow at this time. Notice these points:

1. Jacob had faith. He trusted that God would be with him and protect him.
2. Jacob was modest about what he wanted. He did not ask for fancy clothes or tasty food. Whether God gives us a lot or a little, we should be grateful and use it well.
3. Jacob showed his devotion to God by asking for God’s protection and presence. This is enough to make us comfortable and happy. 

He also promised to stay loyal to God, treating Him as his God in a special agreement. When God blesses us greatly, we should show lots of thankfulness. Giving one-tenth of what we earn is a good amount to set aside for God, although sometimes we might give more or less depending on how God provides for us, 1 Corinthians 16:2. Remember your promises made at important times, like Jacob’s promise at Bethel, where you vowed to serve God fully and dedicate everything to His honor.

29.o: Jacob proceeded cheerfully in his journey, after the sweet communion he had with God at Beth-el. Providence brought him to the field where his uncle's flocks were to be watered. What is said of the care of the shepherds for their sheep, may remind us of the tender concern which our Lord Jesus, the great Shepherd of the sheep, has for his flock the church; for he is the good Shepherd, that knows his sheep, and is known of them. The stone at the well's mouth was to secure it; water was scarce, it was not there for every one's use: but separate interests should not take us from helping one another. When all the shepherds came together with their flocks, then, like loving neighbours, they watered their flocks together. The law of kindness in the tongue has a commanding power, Pr 31:26. Jacob was civil to these strangers, and he found them civil to him.
29.m:
Jacob continued on his journey happily after talking with God at Bethel. 

Providence led him to a field where his uncle’s flocks were getting water. 

What we hear about the shepherds taking care of their sheep reminds us that Jesus, who is called the Great Shepherd, cares for his flock, the church. He is the Good Shepherd who knows his sheep and they know him.

The big rock over the well kept others from using the water. Water was rare, so it wasn’t meant for everyone. But people shouldn't let different needs stop them from helping each other.

When all the shepherds gathered with their flocks, they worked together to give water to all the animals. Kindness with words can get things done, just like Proverbs 31:26 tells us.

Jacob was polite to the strangers, and they were nice back to him.

29.o: See Rachel's humility and industry. Nobody needs to be ashamed of honest, useful labour, nor ought it to hinder any one's preferment. When Jacob understood that this was his kinswoman, he was very ready to serve her. Laban, though not the best humoured, bade him welcome, and was satisfied with the account Jacob gave of himself. While we avoid being foolishly ready to believe every thing which is told us, we must take heed of being uncharitably suspicious.
29.m:
Notice Rachel's hard work and modesty. There's nothing wrong with doing an honest job, and it shouldn't stop anyone from getting ahead. 

When Jacob found out she was his relative, he was happy to help her. Even though Laban wasn't always nice, he welcomed Jacob and believed what Jacob said about himself.

We should be careful not to believe everything we hear, but also not be too skeptical.

29.o: During the month that Jacob spent as a guest, he was not idle. Wherever we are, it is good to employ ourselves in some useful business. Laban was desirous that Jacob should continue with him. Inferior relations must not be imposed upon; it is our duty to reward them. Jacob made known to Laban the affection he had for his daughter Rachel. And having no wordly goods with which to endow her, he promises seven years' service Love makes long and hard services short and easy; hence we read of the labour of love, Heb 6:10. If we know how to value the happiness of heaven, the sufferings of this present time will be as nothing to us. An age of work will be but as a few days to those that love God, and long for Christ's appearing. Jacob, who had imposed upon his father, is imposed upon by Laban, his father-in-law, by a like deception. Herein, how unrighteous soever Laban was, the Lord was righteous: see Jud 1:7. Even the righteous, if they take a false step, are sometimes thus recompensed in the earth. And many who are not, like Jacob, in their marriage, disappointed in person, soon find themselves, as much to their grief, disappointed in the character. The choice of that relation ought to be made with good advice and thought on both sides. There is reason to believe that Laban's excuse was not true. His way of settling the matter made bad worse. Jacob was drawn into the disquiet of multiplying wives. He could not refuse Rachel, for he had espoused her; still less could he refuse Leah. As yet there was no express command against marrying more than one wife. It was in the patriarchs a sin of ignorance; but it will not justify the like practice now, when God's will is plainly made known by the Divine law, Le 18:18, and more fully since, by our Saviour, that one man and woman only must be joined together, 1Co 7:2.
29.m: During the month Jacob stayed with Laban, he didn’t waste his time. We should always use our time wisely wherever we are. Laban wanted Jacob to stay longer. We should treat family members fairly and give them what they deserve. Jacob told Laban about his love for Rachel. Since he had no money to give her, he offered to work for seven years. When you love someone, even hard work feels easy. Long periods of labor seem short when done out of love. Hebrews 6:10 talks about the labor of love. If we treasure heaven’s joy, earthly troubles won't bother us. A lifetime of work seems brief to those who eagerly await Jesus’ return. Jacob, who tricked his father, got tricked by Laban too. This shows that even the righteous face trouble here on Earth. Sometimes people get disappointed in marriage, just like Jacob did. Careful planning and advice are important when choosing a spouse. Laban may have lied about needing time to prepare for Rachel’s wedding. By doing this, he caused Jacob to marry two women instead of one. At first, God hadn’t said anything against having multiple wives. For the early believers, it was an innocent mistake. But today, God clearly tells us through laws and teachings that one man and one woman should be married. Leviticus 18:18 and 1 Corinthians 7:2 confirm this.

29.o: The names Leah gave her children, expressed her respect and regard, both to God and to her husband. Reuben, or See a son, with this thought, Now will my husband love me; Levi, or joined, expecting, Now will my husband be joined unto me. Mutual affection is both the duty and comfort of the married relation; and yoke-fellows should study to recommend themselves to each other, 1Co 7:33, 34. She thankfully acknowledges the kind providence of God in hearing her. Whatever supports and comforts us under afflictions, or tends to our deliverance from them, God must be owned in it. Her fourth son she called Judah, or praise, saying, Now will I praise the Lord. This was he, of whom, as concerning the flesh, Christ came. Whatever is the matter of our rejoicing, ought to be the matter of our thanksgiving. Fresh favours should quicken us to praise God for former favours; Now will I praise the Lord more and better than I have done. All our praises must centre in Christ, both as the matter of them, and as the Mediator of them. He descended after the flesh from him whose name was “Praise,” and He is our praise. Is Christ formed in my heart? Now will I praise the Lord.
29.m:
Leah named her children to show her respect for God and her husband. 

Reuben means "See a son." Leah hoped her husband would now love her. 
Levi means "Joined." Leah expected her husband would join with her in love.

Married people should care about each other and try to make their partners happy. 

She thanked God for answering her prayers. When God helps us during hard times, we should give thanks.

Her fourth child was named Judah, which means "Praise." Leah said, "Now I will praise the Lord."

This child was part of the line where Jesus would come later. We should thank God for good things by praising Him.

New blessings should remind us to praise God even more. Leah wanted to praise God better than before.

All our praise should focus on Christ. He came from the family of Judah, who praised God. And He is our praise too.

Is Christ growing in my heart? Then I will praise the Lord.

30.o: Rachel envied her sister: envy is grieving at the good of another, than which no sin is more hateful to God, or more hurtful to our neighbours and ourselves. She considered not that God made the difference, and that in other things she had the advantage. Let us carefully watch against all the risings and workings of this passion in our minds. Let not our eye be evil towards any of our fellow-servants, because our Master's is good. Jacob loved Rachel, and therefore reproved her for what she said amiss. Faithful reproofs show true affection. God may be to us instead of any creature; but it is sin and folly to place any creature in God's stead, and to place that confidence in any creature, which should be placed in God only. At the persuasion of Rachel, Jacob took Bilhah her handmaid to wife, that, according to the usage of those times, her children might be owned as her mistress's children. Had not Rachel's heart been influenced by evil passions, she would have thought her sister's children nearer to her, and more entitled to her care than Bilhah's. But children whom she had a right to rule, were more desirable to her than children she had more reason to love. As an early instance of her power over these children, she takes pleasure in giving them names that carry in them marks of rivalry with her sister. See what roots of bitterness envy and strife are, and what mischief they make among relations. At the persuasion of Leah, Jacob took Zilpah her handmaid to wife also. See the power of jealousy and rivalship, and admire the wisdom of the Divine appointment, which joins together one man and one woman only; for God hath called us to peace and purity.
30.m:
Rachel was jealous of her sister. Jealousy means feeling sad about someone else’s good fortune, and it’s a very bad sin that hurts others and yourself. She didn’t think about how God caused the differences between them, or that she had advantages too. We need to watch out for feelings of jealousy in our hearts. Our eyes shouldn't be envious toward anyone, since our Master sees everything kindly.

Jacob loved Rachel and told her off gently when she spoke poorly. This shows he cared about her deeply. God can take the place of anything we depend on, but it’s wrong to trust creatures instead of God alone. 

At Rachel’s urging, Jacob married Bilhah, her servant girl. In those days, Bilhah’s kids could be treated as if they belonged to Rachel. If Rachel wasn’t so jealous, she would have cared more for her sister’s children than for Bilhah’s. The children she ruled were more important to her than the ones she should have loved more.

To show her rivalry, Rachel gave Bilhah’s children names meant to compete with her sister’s. Jealousy causes lots of trouble among family members. 

Leah convinced Jacob to marry her servant girl, Zilpah. This shows how much jealousy and competition can control people. It’s amazing how wisely God set up marriage for just one man and one woman, calling us to live peacefully and purely.

30.o: The desire, good in itself, but often too great and irregular, of being the mother of the promised Seed, with the honour of having many children, and the reproach of being barren, were causes of this unbecoming contest between the sisters. The truth appears to be, that they were influenced by the promises of God to Abraham; whose posterity were promised the richest blessings, and from whom the Messiah was to descend.
30.m: The strong wish to be the mother of the promised child, along with the honor of having many children and the shame of being unable to have them, led these sisters to argue. They were motivated by God’s promises to Abraham. His descendants would receive great blessings, and the Messiah would come from his family.

30.o: The fourteen years being gone, Jacob was willing to depart without any provision, except God's promise. But he had in many ways a just claim on Laban's substance, and it was the will of God that he should be provided for from it. He referred his cause to God, rather than agree for stated wages with Laban, whose selfishness was very great. And it would appear that he acted honestly, when none but those of the colours fixed upon should be found among his cattle. Laban selfishly thought that his cattle would produce few different in colour from their own. Jacob's course after this agreement has been considered an instance of his policy and management. But it was done by intimation from God, and as a token of his power. The Lord will one way or another plead the cause of the oppressed, and honour those who simply trust his providence. Neither could Laban complain of Jacob, for he had nothing more than was freely agreed that he should have; nor was he injured, but greatly benefitted by Jacob's services. May all our mercies be received with thanksgiving and prayer, that coming from his bounty, they may lead to his praise.
30.m:
After fourteen years, Jacob wanted to leave without taking anything, relying only on God’s promise. However, he had several reasons to ask for some of Laban’s possessions. It was God’s plan that Jacob receive something from Laban.

Jacob let God decide what was fair instead of agreeing on set wages with Laban, since Laban was very greedy. By making sure only the animals with the special colors were counted, Jacob showed he was honest. 

Laban hoped that most of his animals would stay the same color. After reaching an agreement, Jacob followed God’s guidance and managed things well. This showed God’s power and care. 

God often helps those who are treated unfairly and honors people who trust him fully. Laban couldn’t complain about Jacob because Jacob took only what they both agreed on. In fact, Laban benefited greatly from Jacob’s work.

May we always give thanks and pray over the blessings we receive, recognizing that they come from God and leading us to praise him.

31.o: The affairs of these families are related very minutely, while (what are called) the great events of states and kingdoms at that period, are not mentioned. The Bible teaches people the common duties of life, how to serve God, how to enjoy the blessings he bestows, and to do good in the various stations and duties of life. Selfish men consider themselves robbed of all that goes past them, and covetousness will even swallow up natural affection. Men's overvaluing worldly wealth is that error which is the root of covetousness, envy, and all evil. The men of the world stand in each other's way, and every one seems to be taking away from the rest; hence discontent, envy, and discord. But there are possessions that will suffice for all; happy they who seek them in the first place. In all our removals we should have respect to the command and promise of God. If He be with us, we need not fear. The perils which surround us are so many, that nothing else can really encourage our hearts. To remember favoured seasons of communion with God, is very refreshing when in difficulties; and we should often recollect our vows, that we fail not to fulfil them.
31.m:
The Bible talks about family matters in detail, but doesn't mention big events happening in countries and kingdoms. It shows us how to live well, serving God and enjoying his gifts. People who care only about themselves think they lose out whenever others get something. Greed can take over their love for others. 

Valuing money too much leads to greed, jealousy, and all kinds of bad behavior. Worldly people see each other as rivals, always trying to take more than others have. This causes unhappiness, jealousy, and fights. There are enough resources for everyone if we focus on what truly matters. Those who look for true treasures are happier.

When moving to new places, we should follow God’s commands and promises. With him by our side, we don’t need to worry. Life has many dangers, and only God can give real comfort. Remembering times when we felt close to God helps us feel better during hard times. We should keep our promises to God and try to fulfill them.

31.o: God can put a bridle in the mouth of wicked men, to restrain their malice, though he do not change their hearts. Though they have no love to God's people, they will pretend to it, and try to make a merit of necessity. Foolish Laban! to call those things his gods which could be stolen! Enemies may steal our goods, but not our God. Here Laban lays to Jacob's charge things that he knew not. Those who commit their cause to God, are not forbidden to plead it themselves with meekness and fear. When we read of Rachel's stealing her father's images, what a scene of iniquity opens! The family of Nahor, who left the idolatrous Chaldees; is this family itself become idolatrous? It is even so. The truth seems to be, that they were like some in after-times, who sware by the Lord and by Malcham, Zep 1:5; and like others in our times, who wish to serve both God and mammon. Great numbers will acknowledge the true God in words, but their hearts and houses are the abodes of spiritual idolatry. When a man gives himself up to covetousness, like Laban, the world is his god; and he has only to reside among gross idolaters in order to become one, or at least a favourer of their abominations.
31.m:
God can stop evil people from being too mean, even if He doesn't change their hearts. Wicked people might act nice to get what they want. Laban was foolish to think his idols were worth anything. Thieves can take our stuff, but they can’t take our God. Laban accused Jacob wrongly. People can defend themselves calmly when they trust God. 

When we hear about Rachel taking her dad’s idols, it shows us how sinful the family was. The family of Nahor left the Chaldeans because they worshiped false gods. But now they’re doing the same thing. Some people today say they believe in God but still follow other beliefs. Many claim to know God but keep idols in their homes.

If someone becomes very greedy like Laban, money becomes their god. Living around people who worship false gods makes them support those wrong beliefs too.

31.o: If Jacob were willingly consumed with heat in the day, and frost by night, to become the son-in-law of Laban, what should we refuse to endure, to become the sons of God? Jacob speaks of God as the God of his father; he thought himself unworthy to be regarded, but was beloved for his father's sake. He calls him the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac; for Abraham was dead, and gone to that world where perfect love casts out fear; but Isaac was yet alive, sanctifying the Lord in his heart, as his fear and his dread.
31.m:
If Jacob endured hot days and cold nights to marry Laban's daughter, what should we not endure to become children of God?

Jacob spoke about God as the God of his father. He felt unworthy but was loved because of his father. 

He called God the God of Abraham and the fear of Isaac. Abraham had died and was in heaven without fear. But Isaac was still living and honored God in his heart.

31.o: Laban could neither justify himself nor condemn Jacob, therefore desires to hear no more of that matter. He is not willing to own himself in fault, as he ought to have done. But he proposes a covenant of friendship between them, to which Jacob readily agrees. A heap of stones was raised, to keep up the memory of the event, writing being then not known or little used. A sacrifice of peace offerings was offered. Peace with God puts true comfort into our peace with our friends. They did eat bread together, partaking of the feast upon the sacrifice. In ancient times covenants of friendship were ratified by the parties eating and drinking together. God is judge between contending parties, and he will judge righteously; whoever do wrong, it is at their peril. They gave a new name to the place, The heap of witness. After this angry parley, they part friends. God is often better to us than our fears, and overrules the spirits of men in our favour, beyond what we could have expected; for it is not in vain to trust in him.
31.m:
Laban couldn’t defend himself or blame Jacob, so he didn't want to talk about it anymore. He wouldn’t admit his mistake, even though he should have. Instead, he suggested making a friendship agreement, which Jacob agreed to.

They made a pile of rocks to remember the event since there wasn’t much writing back then. They offered sacrifices to show peace. When we’re at peace with God, it makes our friendships feel good too.

Jacob and Laban ate together from the meal after the sacrifice. Long ago, people would seal agreements by sharing food and drink. 

God watches over those who argue and will decide fairly. Whoever does wrong will face consequences. 

The two named the spot “Heap of Witness.” After their argument, they left as friends. Often, God helps us more than we expect. Trusting Him leads to unexpected blessings.

32.o: The angels of God appeared to Jacob, to encourage him with the assurance of the Divine protection. When God designs his people for great trials, he prepares them by great comforts. While Jacob, to whom the promise belonged, had been in hard service, Esau was become a prince. Jacob sent a message, showing that he did not insist upon the birth-right. Yielding pacifies great offences, Ec 10:4. We must not refuse to speak respectfully, even to those unjustly angry with us. Jacob received an account of Esau's warlike preparations against him, and was greatly afraid. A lively sense of danger, and quickening fear arising from it, may be found united with humble confidence in God's power and promise.
32.m:
God’s angels appeared to Jacob to give him hope that God would protect him. When God wants to test his people, he first gives them comfort. 

While Jacob worked hard, his brother Esau became a powerful ruler. Jacob sent a message saying he didn’t want Esau’s birthright anymore. Giving up what you want can help stop big arguments. Even if someone is angry at you, you should still talk politely to them.

Jacob heard that Esau was getting ready for battle and got very scared. People who trust God can feel both afraid and confident at the same time. They know God will keep his promises and take care of them.

32.o: Times of fear should be times of prayer: whatever causes fear, should drive us to our knees, to our God. Jacob had lately seen his guards of angels, but in this distress he applied to God, not to them; he knew they were his fellow-servants, Re 22:9. There cannot be a better pattern for true prayer than this. Here is a thankful acknowledgement of former undeserved favours; a humble confession of unworthiness; a plain statement of his fears and distress; a full reference of the whole affair to the Lord, and resting all his hopes on him. The best we can say to God in prayer, is what he has said to us. Thus he made the name of the Lord his strong tower, and could not but be safe. Jacob's fear did not make him sink into despair, nor did his prayer make him presume upon God's mercy, without the use of means. God answers prayers by teaching us to order our affairs aright. To pacify Esau, Jacob sent him a present. We must not despair of reconciling ourselves to those most angry against us.
32.m:
When we feel afraid, we should pray. Whatever makes us scared should push us to kneel before God. Jacob had just seen angels guarding him, but now he was in trouble and turned to God instead of the angels. He knew the angels were God’s servants too, like himself. 

Jacob’s way of praying sets a good example for us. First, he thanked God for past kindnesses. Then, he admitted he didn’t deserve those blessings. Next, he clearly told God about his worries and troubles. Finally, he put everything in God’s hands and trusted Him completely.

The best things to say in prayer are the promises God has already given us. By doing this, Jacob made God’s name a strong shelter for him, so he felt safe. His fear didn't stop him from being hopeful, and his prayers didn’t make him think God would save him without him trying to solve problems first.

God often answers our prayers by guiding us to handle situations wisely. To calm down Esau, who was very upset, Jacob gave him gifts. This shows we shouldn’t give up hope of making peace with people who are really mad at us.

32.o: A great while before day, Jacob being alone, more fully spread his fears before God in prayer. While thus employed, One in the likeness of a man wrestled with him. When the spirit helpeth our infirmities, and our earnest and vast desires can scarcely find words to utter them, and we still mean more than we can express, then prayer is indeed wrestling with God. However tried or discouraged, we shall prevail; and prevailing with Him in prayer, we shall prevail against all enemies that strive with us. Nothing requires more vigour and unceasing exertion than wrestling. It is an emblem of the true spirit of faith and prayer. Jacob kept his ground; though the struggle continued long, this did not shake his faith, nor silence his prayer. He will have a blessing, and had rather have all his bone put out of joint than go away without one. Those who would have the blessing of Christ, must resolve to take no denial. The fervent prayer is the effectual prayer. The Angel puts a lasting mark of honour upon him, by changing his name. Jacob signifies a supplanter. From henceforth he shall be celebrated, not for craft and artful management, but for true valour. Thou shalt be called Israel, a prince with God, a name greater than those of the great men of the earth. He is a prince indeed that is a prince with God; those are truly honourable that are mighty in prayer. Having power with God, he shall have power with men too; he shall prevail, and gain Esau's favour. Jacob gives a new name to the place. He calls it Peniel, the face of God, because there he had seen the appearance of God, and obtained the favour of God. It becomes those whom God honours, to admire his grace towards them. The Angel who wrestled with Jacob was the second Person in the sacred Trinity, who was afterwards God manifest in the flesh, and who, dwelling in human nature, is called Immanuel, Ho 12:4, 5. Jacob halted on his thigh. It might serve to keep him from being lifted up with the abundance of the revelations. The sun rose on Jacob: it is sun-rise with that soul, which has had communion with God.
32.m:
Before dawn, Jacob prayed alone, pouring out his worries to God. As he prayed, someone appeared in the form of a man and wrestled with him. When we pray deeply, struggling to express what’s in our hearts, it’s like wrestling with God. Even if we’re tested or feel down, we’ll win if we keep praying. Wrestling takes strength and constant effort—like real faith and prayer do. Jacob stayed firm during their fight. Though it lasted a long time, he didn’t give up. He wanted a blessing so much that he’d rather lose part of himself than leave empty-handed. To get God’s blessing, we need to refuse to accept “no.” Strong prayers work best. 

The angel gave Jacob a special honor by giving him a new name. Before, he was called Jacob, which means “supplanter,” but now he’s called Israel, which means “prince with God”—a better title. True greatness comes from being strong in prayer. With God’s help, Jacob could handle anything, even winning over Esau. Jacob named the place where he met God Peniel, which means “face of God,” because he saw God there and received His favor. People should remember God’s kindness toward them.

This angel was actually Jesus, the Son of God, who came to Earth later. Jacob’s leg hurt after the wrestling match. This may have helped him stay humble despite his victory. For Jacob, the sun rising meant his soul was starting a new day filled with God’s presence.

33.o: Jacob, having by prayer committed his case to God, went on his way. Come what will, nothing can come amiss to him whose heart is fixed, trusting in God. Jacob bowed to Esau. A humble, submissive behaviour goes far towards turning away wrath. Esau embraced Jacob. God has the hearts of all men in his hands, and can turn them when and how he pleases. It is not in vain to trust in God, and to call upon him in the day of trouble. And when a man's ways please the Lord he maketh even his enemies to be at peace with him. Esau receives Jacob as a brother, and much tenderness passes between them. Esau asks, Who are those with thee? To this common question, Jacob spoke like himself, like a man whose eyes are ever directed towards the Lord. Jacob urged Esau, though his fear was over, and he took his present. It is well when men's religion makes them generous, free-hearted, and open-handed. But Jacob declined Esau's offer to accompany him. It is not desirable to be too intimate with superior ungodly relations, who will expect us to join in their vanities, or at least to wink at them, though they blame, and perhaps mock at, our religion. Such will either be a snare to us, or offended with us. We shall venture the loss of all things, rather than endanger our souls, if we know their value; rather than renounce Christ, if we truly love him. And let Jacob's care and tender attention to his family and flocks remind us of the good Shepherd of our souls, who gathers the lambs with his arm, and carries them in his bosom, and gently leads those that are with young, Isa 40:11. As parents, teachers or pastors, we should all follow his example.
33.m:
Jacob prayed and then continued on his journey. Whatever happens next won't bother him because he trusts God completely. 

When Jacob met Esau, he bowed down humbly. This kind behavior helped ease any anger Esau might have felt. 

Esau hugged Jacob warmly. God controls people’s hearts and can soften them when He wants to. Trusting God helps in times of trouble. When someone pleases God, He can even bring peace between enemies.

Esau welcomed Jacob like a brother, showing lots of kindness. Esau asked about the people with Jacob. Jacob answered honestly, just like someone who always looks to God for guidance.

Even after feeling less worried, Jacob still gave gifts to Esau. Religion should make people generous and willing to share freely. However, Jacob didn’t accept Esau’s offer to travel together. Being close to ungodly relatives could lead to problems. They might want you to join in their wrongdoings or ignore your faith. That would put your soul in danger. You’d rather lose everything else than give up your faith in Christ.

Remember Jacob’s care for his family and animals. Think of Jesus, the Good Shepherd who takes care of His sheep. He picks up the weak ones and carries them safely. He treats the others gently too. Parents, teachers, and leaders should follow Jesus’ example.

33.o: Jacob did not content himself with words of thanks for God's favour to him, but gave real thanks. Also he kept up religion, and the worship of God in his family. Where we have a tent, God must have an altar. Jacob dedicated this altar to the honour of El-elohe-Israel, God, the God of Israel; to the honour of God, the only living and true God; and to the honour of the God of Israel, as a God in covenant with him. Israel's God is Israel's glory. Blessed be his name, he is still the mighty God, the God of Israel. May we praise his name, and rejoice in his love, through our pilgrimage here on earth, and for ever in the heavenly Canaan.
33.m:
Jacob didn't just say thank you to God for being kind to him. He showed his gratitude by doing something meaningful. 

He also made sure to keep up religious practices and worship in his family. No matter where they set up their tents, God needed an altar too. 

Jacob named this altar after El-elohe-Israel, which means God, the God of Israel. This was to honor God, the one true and living God, and the God who had a special promise with him. 

The God of Israel is the pride of Israel. We should always praise God’s name and celebrate his love during our time on Earth and forever in heaven.

34.o: Young persons, especially females, are never so safe and well off as under the care of pious parents. Their own ignorance, and the flattery and artifices of designing, wicked people, who are ever laying snares for them, expose them to great danger. They are their own enemies if they desire to go abroad, especially alone, among strangers to true religion. Those parents are very wrong who do not hinder their children from needlessly exposing themselves to danger. Indulged children, like Dinah, often become a grief and shame to their families. Her pretence was, to see the daughters of the land, to see how they dressed, and how they danced, and what was fashionable among them; she went to see, yet that was not all, she went to be seen too. She went to get acquaintance with the Canaanites, and to learn their ways. See what came of Dinah's gadding. The beginning of sin is as the letting forth of water. How great a matter does a little fire kindle! We should carefully avoid all occasions of sin and approaches to it.
34.m:
Young people, especially girls, are safest and best cared for by good parents. Their lack of knowledge and the tricks of bad people who want to harm them put them at risk. It’s dangerous for young people to go out on their own, especially around those who don’t follow true religion. Parents who let their kids put themselves in unnecessary danger are making mistakes. Spoiled children, like Dinah, often bring trouble and shame to their families. 

Dinah said she wanted to see how other girls lived and dressed. But her real goal was to show herself off too. She visited the Canaanite women to learn their customs. Look what happened because of Dinah’s wandering. Sin starts small, like opening a floodgate. A tiny spark can start a big fire. We should stay away from anything that might lead us to sin.

34.o: The Shechemites submitted to the sacred rite, only to serve a turn, to please their prince, and to enrich themselves, and it was just with God to bring punishment upon them. As nothing secures us better than true religion, so nothing exposes us more than religion only pretended to. But Simeon and Levi were most unrighteous. Those who act wickedly, under the pretext of religion, are the worst enemies of the truth, and harden the hearts of many to destruction. The crimes of others form no excuse for us. Alas! how one sin leads on to another, and, like flames of fire, spread desolation in every direction! Foolish pleasures lead to seduction; seduction produces wrath; wrath thirsts for revenge; the thirst of revenge has recourse to treachery; treachery issues in murder; and murder is followed by other lawless actions. Were we to trace the history of unlawful commerce between the sexes, we should find it, more than any other sin, ending in blood.
34.m:
The people of Shechem went through the religious ceremony just to please their leader and get richer. It was right for God to punish them. True religion protects us, but pretending to be religious makes us vulnerable.

Simeon and Levi did wrong things using religion as an excuse. People who do evil while saying they follow religion are the biggest enemies of truth. They make others reject the truth too. 

We cannot use other people’s sins as excuses for our own bad behavior. One sin often leads to more sins. Pleasure turns to temptation, which causes anger. Anger leads to wanting revenge, then comes trickery, and finally murder. Murder leads to even more illegal acts.

If we look at how people have broken sexual laws, we see it usually ends up causing violence.

35.o: Beth-el was forgotten. But as many as God loves, he will remind of neglected duties, one way or other, by conscience or by providences. When we have vowed a vow to God, it is best not to defer the payment of it; yet better late than never. Jacob commanded his household to prepare, not only for the journey and removal, but for religious services. Masters of families should use their authority to keep up religion in their families, Jos 24:15. They must put away strange gods. In families where there is a face of religion, and an altar to God, yet many times there is much amiss, and more strange gods than one would suppose. They must be clean, and change their garments. These were but outward ceremonies, signifying the purifying and change of the heart. What are clean clothes, and new clothes, without a clean heart, and a new heart? If Jacob had called for these idols sooner, they had parted with them sooner. Sometimes attempts for reformation succeed better than we could have thought. Jacob buried their images. We must be wholly separated from our sins, as we are from those that are dead and buried out of sight. He removed from Shechem to Beth-el. Though the Canaanites were very angry against the sons of Jacob for their barbarous usage of the Shechemites, yet they were so kept back by Divine power, that they could not take the opportunity now offered to avenge them. The way of duty is the way of safety. When we are about God's work, we are under special protection; God is with us, while we are with him; and if He be for us, who can be against us? God governs the world more by secret terrors on men's minds than we are aware of.
35.m:
Bethel was forgotten. But God reminds people he loves about their promises, either through their conscience or life events. It’s best not to delay paying vows to God, though even being late is better than never doing it at all. 

Jacob told his family to get ready for both the trip and worship. Family leaders should use their influence to support faith within their households, just like Joshua did. They needed to leave behind foreign gods. Even homes that look religious often hide hidden problems, including multiple false gods. 

They also had to wash themselves and wear fresh clothes. This showed they wanted to cleanse their hearts too. Clean and new clothing means nothing without a pure heart. If Jacob had gotten rid of the idols earlier, it might have been easier. Sometimes efforts at fixing things go better than expected.

He buried the family’s idols. To truly turn from sin, we need to completely separate ourselves from it, like burying what’s dead and gone. Jacob moved from Shechem to Bethel. Despite the Canaanites’ anger over what Jacob’s sons did to the Shechemites, God prevented them from attacking. Following God keeps us safe. When we do God’s work, he protects us. People may fear us because of unseen divine warnings.

35.o: The comfort the saints have in holy ordinances, is not so much from Beth-el, the house of God, as from El-beth-el, the God of the house. The ordinances are empty things, if we do not meet with God in them. There Jacob buried Deborah, Rebekah's nurse. She died much lamented. Old servants in a family, that have in their time been faithful and useful, ought to be respected. God appeared to Jacob. He renewed the covenant with him. I am God Almighty, God all-sufficient, able to make good the promise in due time, and to support thee and provide for thee in the mean time. Two things are promised; that he should be the father of a great nation, and that he should be the master of a good land. These two promises had a spiritual signification, which Jacob had some notion of, though not so clear and distinct as we now have. Christ is the promised Seed, and heaven is the promised land; the former is the foundation, and the latter the top-stone, of all God's favours.
35.m:
The comfort that believers find in religious ceremonies comes more from meeting God than just being at a place called the House of God. The services themselves don’t matter much unless we connect with God during them.

Deborah, who was Rebekah’s nurse, died here and was mourned greatly. Older servants who were once loyal and helpful deserve respect.

God spoke to Jacob again. He made a new agreement with him. “I am God All-Powerful,” he said. “I will keep my promise eventually and take care of you until then.”

Two things were promised: that Jacob would become the ancestor of many people and that he would live in a good land. 

These promises had deeper meanings that Jacob understood somewhat, even if not clearly. Jesus is the promised descendant, and heaven is the promised land. One is the base, the other the peak, of all God’s blessings.

35.o: Rachel had passionately said, Give me children, or else I die; and now that she had children, she died! The death of the body is but the departure of the soul to the world of spirits. When shall we learn that it is God alone who really knows what is best for his people, and that in all worldly affairs the safest path for the Christian is to say from the heart, It is the Lord, let him do what seemeth him good. Here alone is our safety and our comfort, to know no will but his. Her dying lips called her newborn son Ben-oni, the son of my sorrow; and many a son proves to be the heaviness of her that bare him. Children are enough the sorrow of their mothers; they should, therefore, when they grow up, study to be their joy, and so, if possible, to make them some amends. But Jacob, because he would not renew the sorrowful remembrance of the mother's death every time he called his son, changed his name to Benjamin, the son of my right hand: that is, very dear to me; the support of my age, like the staff in my right hand.
35.m:
Rachel once said, “Give me children or I will die.” And after having children, she did die!

The death of the body means the soul goes to the spirit world. We need to remember that only God truly knows what’s best for us. For Christians, the safest way is to trust God by saying, “It is the Lord, let him do what seems good.”

Our safety and happiness come from knowing only God’s will. 

As Rachel was dying, she named her newborn son Ben-oni, which means “son of my sorrow.” Many times, children bring sadness to their mothers. So when grown-up children see this, they should try to become a source of joy for their parents.

But Jacob didn’t want to think about his mother’s death each time he spoke of his son. He renamed the boy Benjamin, which means “son of my right hand,” showing how much he loved him and saw him as a great help in old age.

35.o: What a sore affliction Reuben's sin was, is shown, “and Israel heard it.” No more is said, but that is enough. Reuben thought that his father would never hear of it; but those that promise themselves secrecy in sin, are generally disappointed. The age and death of Isaac are recorded, though he died not till after Joseph was sold into Egypt. Isaac lived about forty years after he had made his will, chap. 27:2. We shall not die an hour the sooner, but much the better, for timely setting our hearts and houses in order. Particular notice is taken of the agreement of Esau and Jacob at their father's funeral, to show how God had wonderfully changed Esau's mind. It is awful to behold relations, sometimes for a little of this world's goods, disputing over the graves of their friends, while they are near going to the grave themselves.
35.m:
Reuben's sin caused him great trouble, and "Israel heard about it." That’s all we need to know. Reuben hoped his father wouldn’t find out, but people who think their sins will stay secret often get caught.

The Bible mentions Isaac’s age and death even though he didn't pass away until long after Joseph was sold into slavery. Isaac lived another forty years after making his will in chapter 27:2. Setting things in order early helps us live better lives.

Jacob and Esau agreed on their father’s funeral arrangements. This shows how God changed Esau’s attitude. It’s sad to see family members fighting over money from their loved ones’ estates while they themselves may soon be gone too.

37.o: In Joseph's history we see something of Christ, who was first humbled and then exalted. It also shows the lot of Christians, who must through many tribulations enter into the kingdom. It is a history that has none like it, for displaying the various workings of the human mind, both good and bad, and the singular providence of God in making use of them for fulfilling his purposes. Though Joseph was his father's darling, yet he was not bred up in idleness. Those do not truly love their children, who do not use them to business, and labour, and hardships. The fondling of children is with good reason called the spoiling of them. Those who are trained up to do nothing, are likely to be good for nothing. But Jacob made known his love, by dressing Joseph finer than the rest of his children. It is wrong for parents to make a difference between one child and another, unless there is great cause for it, by the children's dutifulness, or undutifulness. When parents make a difference, children soon notice it, and it leads to quarrels in families. Jacob's sons did that, when they were from under his eye, which they durst not have done at home with him; but Joseph gave his father an account of their ill conduct, that he might restrain them. Not as a tale-bearer, to sow discord, but as a faithful brother.
37.m:
Joseph’s story tells us about Jesus, who was first humbled and then raised up. It also shows what happens to Christians—they face many troubles before entering heaven. This history is unique because it shows all kinds of thoughts and actions, both good and bad, and how God uses them to fulfill His plans.

Even though Joseph was his dad’s favorite, he wasn’t spoiled. Parents who don't let their kids work or struggle aren't really helping them. Spoiling children often makes them useless later on. Jacob showed his love by giving Joseph nicer clothes than the other kids. It’s unfair for parents to treat children differently unless there’s a good reason, like being more responsible or less so. 

When Jacob’s sons were away, they did things they wouldn’t dare do if their dad was around. They fought and caused trouble. Joseph told his dad about their misbehavior so he could stop them. He didn’t gossip or spread rumors—he just wanted to help keep peace in the family.

37.o: God gave Joseph betimes the prospect of his advancement, to support and comfort him under his long and grievous troubles. Observe, Joseph dreamed of his preferment, but he did not dream of his imprisonment. Thus many young people, when setting out in the world, think of nothing but prosperity and pleasure, and never dream of trouble. His brethren rightly interpreted the dream, though they abhorred the interpretation of it. While they committed crimes in order to defeat it, they were themselves the instruments of accomplishing it. Thus the Jews understood what Christ said of his kingdom. Determined that he should not reign over them, they consulted to put him to death; and by his crucifixion, made way for the exaltation they designed to prevent.
37.m:
God showed Joseph early on that he would be successful one day. This helped him stay strong during all his hard times. 

Joseph had dreams about becoming important, but he didn’t dream about going to jail. Many young people starting their lives only think about good things and fun. They don't imagine facing problems.

Joseph’s brothers correctly guessed what his dreams meant, even if they hated the idea. When they tried to stop Joseph from becoming powerful, they ended up helping him succeed anyway. The Jewish leaders knew what Jesus was saying about his rule. They wanted to keep him from being king, so they planned to kill him. But by putting him to death, they actually helped bring about the very thing they feared.

37.o: How readily does Joseph wait his father's orders! Those children who are best beloved by their parents, should be the most ready to obey them. See how deliberate Joseph's brethren were against him. They thought to slay him from malice aforethought, and in cold blood. Whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer, 1Jo 3:15. The sons of Jacob hated their brother because their father loved him. New occasions, as his dreams and the like, drew them on further; but this laid rankling in their hearts, till they resolved on his death. God has all hearts in his hands.
